data.text: I’ve confirmed the frontend e2e area and there’s already a fixture scaffold. Next I’m locating any existing scale or performance-related tests so I can see whether this bead is a simple additive fixture/measurable-baseline task or if the repo already has the measurement harness I need to reuse.

data.output:
# Playwright e2e fixture workspace

This directory provides a self-contained DDx workspace that the default
Playwright harness copies into a temp directory before booting the Go DDx
server. The fixture isolates e2e runs from the developer's home directory and
from the repository's live `.ddx/` state.

Contents:

- `.ddx/config.yaml` — minimal DDx config pointing at the local plugin library.
- `.ddx/beads.jsonl` — open, closed, and blocked beads so the bead API endpoints
  return non-empty data.
- `.ddx/plugins/ddx/` — minimal personas, prompts, and templates so the document
  and persona endpoints have something to list.
- `docs/` — small docs library for the document graph endpoint.

The fixture is read-only; the harness copies it to a `mktemp -d` workspace at
boot so test runs do not mutate these files. Do not reference user-home paths
from anything in this directory.

docs/helix/00-discover/product-vision.md:36:> Software is leverage. Fifty years of practice has shown that there are physics of software that must be respected to produce and maintain systems at scale. The agentic era allows every team to produce systems at scale and makes this even more important.
docs/helix/00-discover/references/REF-001-spec-driven.md:36:Throughout this specification process, research agents gather critical context. They investigate library compatibility, performance benchmarks, and security implications. Organizational constraints are discovered and applied automatically—your company's database standards, authentication requirements, and deployment policies seamlessly integrate into every specification.
docs/helix/00-discover/references/REF-001-spec-driven.md:66:**Research-Driven Context**: Research agents gather critical context throughout the specification process, investigating technical options, performance implications, and organizational constraints.
docs/helix/00-discover/references/REF-001-spec-driven.md:70:**Branching for Exploration**: Generate multiple implementation approaches from the same specification to explore different optimization targets—performance, maintainability, user experience, cost.

docs/helix/00-discover/references/REF-008-lost-in-the-middle.md:12:summary: "Empirical study showing LLM performance degrades sharply when relevant information sits in the middle of long contexts, with U-shaped recall curves favoring start and end positions."
docs/helix/00-discover/references/REF-009-context-rot.md:11:summary: "2025 study quantifying performance degradation as prompt length grows, even within advertised context windows; effect persists across frontier models."
docs/helix/00-discover/references/REF-010-levels-of-automation.md:12:summary: "1978 technical report introducing the canonical 10-level scale of human–computer automation, from full manual to full autonomy, foundational to human-in-the-loop design."
docs/helix/00-discover/references/REF-013-mast-multi-agent-failures.md:17:Empirical study cataloguing how multi-agent LLM systems fail in practice — most failures are coordination/verification breakdowns, not raw model capability gaps. Justifies DDx's emphasis on explicit handoffs (beads), clear acceptance criteria, and review gates over loosely-coordinated agent swarms.

docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:9:# ADR-006: Use Tailscale ts-net for Network Authentication
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:16:chose Tailscale ts-net for its zero-configuration identity model.
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:20:Use **Tailscale's tsnet library** (`tailscale.com/tsnet`) to provide
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:28:| **ts-net (Tailscale)** | **Chosen** | Identity from Tailscale network; zero config for users already on a tailnet; mutual TLS built in; no secrets to manage |
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:58:1. Joins the user's tailnet using their Tailscale identity
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:59:2. Receives a machine certificate from the Tailscale coordination server
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:60:3. Serves HTTPS with automatic Let's Encrypt certificates via Tailscale
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:61:4. Identifies callers by their Tailscale identity (node name, user email)
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:65:Every request through the ts-net listener carries Tailscale identity headers:
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:75:full access. Access control via Tailscale ACLs is a future refinement.
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:80:import "tailscale.com/tsnet"
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:106:| `server.tsnet.hostname` | string | "ddx" | Tailscale hostname |
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:107:| `server.tsnet.auth_key` | string | "" | Tailscale auth key (for headless/CI); see precedence note below |
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:109:| `TS_AUTHKEY` | env var | unset | Tailscale auth key (preferred; avoids secrets in config files or shell history) |
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:128:  Identity comes from Tailscale.
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:129:- **Tailscale dependency**: Users who want remote access must have Tailscale
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:130:  installed. This is acceptable for a developer tool — Tailscale is widely
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-006-tsnet-authentication.md:136:- **Automatic HTTPS**: ts-net provides TLS certificates via Tailscale's
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-007-federation-topology.md:102:- mDNS / Tailscale-tag auto-discovery is **rejected for v1**: it couples DDx to
docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-007-federation-topology.md:103:  Tailscale ACL policy and adds a discovery surface we don't need yet.

docs/helix/02-design/efficacy-compare-redesign.md:9:The Efficacy page had a standalone "Compare" button that opened an empty modal asking the user to add "arms" (model + prompt pairs) from scratch. User feedback: the button's purpose was not obvious — the Efficacy page aggregates sessions by `(harness, provider, model)`, so a cold-start "design an A/B experiment" flow sitting next to it created a mental-model mismatch.
docs/helix/02-design/efficacy-compare-redesign.md:11:The backing feature (`Query.comparisons`, `Mutation.comparisonDispatch`, `ComparisonArmInput`) is a real A/B capability. It is not wrong to have — it is wrong to expose as a cold-start modal colocated with a rollup view.
docs/helix/02-design/efficacy-compare-redesign.md:18:- **Usage check.** No `.ddx/comparisons/` directory exists in this repo; the bead index has no `comparison_id` correlation. The cold-start flow has not been exercised in practice. This supports replacing it rather than preserving parity.
docs/helix/02-design/efficacy-compare-redesign.md:24:The Efficacy page gains a leading checkbox column on the rollup table. Selecting 2+ rows enables a "Compare selected" header button that dispatches a comparison pre-seeded with the selected rows' `(harness, provider, model)` tuples. The user supplies only the prompt. The cold-start empty-modal button is removed.
docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-04.md:917:| R-008 | MiniSearch performance with large bead sets | Low | Low | ADR-005 v2 uses plain JS arrays + MiniSearch; SQLite-WASM deployment complexity no longer applies; future mitigation: server-side pagination |

Audit event includes identity.\n  - resolver_operator_prompt.go:218-235 appends a BeadEvent of kind\n    \"operator_prompt_submit\" whose Body records identity kind, RemoteAddr,\n    X-Tailscale-User, X-Tailscale-Node, and idempotency_key, and whose Actor\n    is set from operatorPromptIdentity (tsnet user OR localhost:<remote>).\n  - Test: TestOperatorPromptSubmit_HappyPath asserts EventsByKind returns\n    exactly one event with non-empty Actor and Body containing identity= and\n    idempotency_key=key-1.\n\nAC5. Default = proposed (not ready).\n  - bead.NewOperatorPromptBead (internal/bead/types.go:163) sets\n    Status = StatusProposed and IssueType = IssueTypeOperatorPrompt.\n  - Test: TestOperatorPromptSubmit_HappyPath asserts Status == StatusProposed\n    and IssueType == IssueTypeOperatorPrompt.\n\nAC6. Tests cover all five.\n  - cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_operator_prompt_test.go has four\n    tests (HappyPath, CSRF with 3 subtests, Idempotency, TTLEviction) which\n    together exercise AC1–AC5.\n\nVerification\n------------\n\n  $ go test ./internal/server/graphql/ -run 'OperatorPrompt|MemoryIdempotency' -v -count=1\n  === RUN   TestOperatorPromptSubmit_HappyPath\n  --- PASS: TestOperatorPromptSubmit_HappyPath\n  === RUN   TestOperatorPromptSubmit_CSRF\n      --- PASS: missing_header_rejected\n      --- PASS: wrong_token_rejected\n      --- PASS: valid_token_accepted\n  === RUN   TestOperatorPromptSubmit_Idempotency\n  --- PASS: TestOperatorPromptSubmit_Idempotency\n  === RUN   TestMemoryIdempotencyCache_TTLEviction\n  --- PASS: TestMemoryIdempotencyCache_TTLEviction\n  PASS\n  ok  github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/internal/server/graphql 0.028s\n\nConclusion\n----------\n\nNo code changes are warranted. The bead's contract is fully met by code\nalready present at HEAD. Re-running the review against the current tree\n(rather than a single attempt's diff) should resolve the BLOCK.\nresult_rev=9cc2428053e792c509cb6db73762c898fe9fd249\nbase_rev=9cc2428053e792c509cb6db73762c898fe9fd249","created_at":"2026-05-02T14:30:36.953884933Z","kind":"execute-bead","source":"ddx agent execute-loop","summary":"already_satisfied"}

docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-030-axon-bead-backend.md:56:| D2 | **Auth**: localhost-only by default; ts-net for remote. | Reuses ADR-006 (tsnet authentication). No new auth machinery in DDx. Axon already supports ts-net in `crates/axon-server/src/serve.rs` (Tailscale mode + LocalAPI whois + role tags), so the integration is configuration-only on both sides. |
docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-030-axon-bead-backend.md:57:| D3 | **Events storage**: separate `ddx_bead_events` collection with `event_of` links (Option B). | Two collections: `ddx_beads` (the bead row) and `ddx_bead_events` (one entity per event). Bead row stays light; events scale per-bead independently. |
docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-030-axon-bead-backend.md:59:| D5 | **Wire transport**: GraphQL only. | All DDx↔Axon traffic is GraphQL — queries and mutations over HTTP, subscriptions over WebSocket. No gRPC, no REST, no MCP from the DDx side. The Go client is generated by `genqlient` from a pinned schema-introspection snapshot. Rationale: Axon's GraphQL surface already covers entity CRUD, links, traversal, transactions, schema, and subscriptions; DDx is already a GraphQL server internally (`cli/internal/server/graphql_*.go`), so DDx↔Axon is wire-symmetric; subscriptions unlock live UI updates for `ddx work` (Story 8) without polling; no protoc/codegen toolchain enters the DDx build. The per-call wire-overhead advantage of gRPC is immeasurable at DDx scale (low thousands of beads); if batch-transaction throughput later becomes painful for one specific path, gRPC can be added there without revisiting this decision. |

Day 2 (after Axon's `axon-05c1019d` pattern-query support ships): single GraphQL query against an `isReady` computed/`@cypher` field. .ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:5:{"acceptance":"- [ ] New default wall-clock deadline (e.g. 3h) applied to both `OSExecutor.ExecuteInDir` and `Runner.RunAgent`\n- [ ] Wall-clock timeout fires even when stdout/stderr/agent events are flowing (unit test: fake provider emits 1 event/sec for \u003e deadline, expect cancel at deadline ± 1s)\n- [ ] Idle timeout behaviour preserved (unit test: no activity for idle timeout → fire)\n- [ ] Result.json distinguishes `timeout: wall-clock` from `timeout: idle`\n- [ ] Documented in types.go (DefaultWallClockMS constant with comment)","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T05:15:25Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1833233","closing_commit_sha":"fff2c86240b98cd687a9dc9879926b17411db3f5","created_at":"2026-04-18T04:41:40.15942901Z","description":"## Root cause RC2 from ddx-0a651925\n\nThe `DefaultTimeoutMS = 7200000` (2h) in `cli/internal/agent/types.go:363` is advertised as a hard cap but implemented as an *idle timeout*:\n\n- `cli/internal/agent/executor.go:154-178` resets `time.NewTimer(idleTimeout)` on every stdout/stderr byte.\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:264-285` resets on every non-compaction agentlib event.\n\nA provider that emits any heartbeat, retry line, or periodic event defeats the timer indefinitely — enabling the 33–142h hangs observed on agent projects.\n\nOnly the claude streaming path (`cli/internal/agent/claude_stream.go:373-384`) uses a true wall-clock deadline via `time.After(timeout)`. That path is why bundle `20260415T015231-c2ffa9ba` fired at exactly 2h 0m 0.098s — and nothing else does.\n\n## Scope\n\n1. Add an absolute wall-clock deadline in addition to (not replacing) the idle timeout.\n2. Default the wall-clock bound to something operators can trust — e.g. 3x `DefaultTimeoutMS` or a new `DefaultWallClockMS`.\n3. Apply in: `OSExecutor.ExecuteInDir` (subprocess path) and `Runner.RunAgent` (embedded agent path).\n4. Reflect the wall-clock error in result.json as a distinct `error: \"wall-clock deadline exceeded after Xh\"` so it is not confused with idle timeout.\n\n## Non-goals\n\n- Lowering or removing the existing idle timeout.\n- Per-provider tuning (can be follow-up).","events_attachment":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-b203c889/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-18T05:29:25.624987414Z","id":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-0a651925-20260418T043148-1346e8a3-b203c889","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:bug","area:agent","area:workers","root-cause:rc2"],"owner":"erik","parent":"ddx-0a651925","priority":0,"session_id":"eb-0acc00fd","status":"closed","title":"execute-bead: add wall-clock deadline alongside idle timeout for agent harness","updated_at":"2026-04-18T05:30:49.793036993Z"}
.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:54:{"acceptance":"File exists at skills/ddx/reference/beads.md. Contains sections covering: inline context requirement, explicit file scope (in + out), small-bead rule of thumb, command-based acceptance criteria, tests-included requirement, rollback expectations, spec-id guidance, CLI reference. No occurrences of 'phase:(frame|design|test|build|deploy|iterate|review)' in body. No mandatory 'helix' label requirement (grep -E 'helix.*(required|must|required)' returns no matches). Linked from skills/ddx/SKILL.md intent-router.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","created_at":"2026-04-17T22:05:31.000159751Z","description":"Create skills/ddx/reference/beads.md — the domain reference file a harness reads when the user asks to create, plan, or update beads.\n\nContent required:\n- **Inline context, not links.** Every bead description must be readable cold by an automated execute-bead worker that has no chat history and no open tabs.\n- **Explicit in-scope file list.** Repo-relative paths. Bad: 'the auth module'. Good: 'cli/internal/auth/pipeline.go and cli/internal/auth/pipeline_test.go'.\n- **Explicit do-NOT-touch scope negation** (non-goals). Prevents scope creep during agent execution.\n- **Favor many small beads.** Rule of thumb: if a bead would touch more than ~3 files or more than one subsystem, decompose into child beads under an epic.\n- **Acceptance is a command that passes, not a sentence.** 'X works' is not AC. 'cd cli \u0026\u0026 go test ./internal/foo/... passes and returns 0' is AC.\n- **Implementations include tests.** The acceptance criteria must reference a test command.\n- **Rollback/cleanup expectations** for partial changes — what 'leave it unclaimed' means in practice.\n- **spec-id linkage** when a governing artifact exists (FEAT-*, SD-*, TD-*, ADR-*); omit when it doesn't.\n- **CLI reference** at the end: ddx bead create/update/close/list/show/ready/blocked/dep/import/export/evidence.\n\nSource material: existing /skills/ddx-bead/SKILL.md (already contains much of this — especially the top-level \"Execution-Ready\" section added in an earlier refinement). Strip HELIX-specific rules: remove the mandatory 'helix' label and the phase:frame|design|test|build|deploy|iterate|review enumeration. Keep generic guidance ('label beads with area, kind, and any workflow tags your project uses').\n\nIn-scope files:\n- skills/ddx/reference/beads.md (new)\n\nOut-of-scope:\n- skills/ddx/SKILL.md (separate bead ddx-bd852e86)\n- skills/ddx/reference/{work,review,agents,status}.md (separate beads)","id":"ddx-0827da45","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:implementation","area:skills"],"parent":"ddx-f586b4fd","priority":1,"spec-id":"FEAT-011","status":"closed","title":"Create skills/ddx/reference/beads.md — execution-ready bead authoring","updated_at":"2026-04-17T22:23:03.141018211Z"}
.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:61:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer reviewing how different harness/provider/model combinations are performing, I expect the Efficacy page to load quickly on a workspace with tens of thousands of sessions, and I expect the rows to reflect every agent invocation — not just those tied to closed beads.\n\n**Acceptance criteria:**\n\n1. **Source change.** `EfficacyRows` resolver reads from the sharded sessions index (`.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, per `ddx-2ceb02fa`). Bead-event read paths (`efficacyFingerprint`, `buildEfficacySnapshot`'s event loop, `attemptsFromEvidence`, `efficacyMemo`) are removed in the same PR — no dead code retained.\n\n2. **Schema parity.** Row shape and field names stay as they are today (rowKey, harness, provider, model, attempts, successes, successRate, medianInputTokens, medianOutputTokens, medianDurationMs, medianCostUsd, warning). UI does not need to change. Optional new filters (`since`, `until`, `projectId`) are added as nullable args with sensible defaults.\n\n3. **Fixture + baseline recorded.** Fixture seeds ≥10,000 session index rows across 2+ monthly shards with ≥10 distinct `(harness, provider, model)` groups. Baseline latencies (before any optimization beyond source change) written to bead notes with hardware + go-version footer.\n\n4. **First-pass perf targets.**\n   - All-time rollup on 10k-row, 2-shard fixture: p95 ≤ 150ms in-process / ≤ 400ms HTTP.\n   - Last-30-days rollup on same fixture: p95 ≤ 60ms / ≤ 200ms HTTP.\n   - Stretch fixture (50k rows × 24 shards): all-time p95 ≤ 400ms in-process / ≤ 1000ms HTTP, or **user is consulted** before choosing a new substrate.\n\n5. **DB-decision protocol (per user directive 2026-04-22).**\n   - If targets in (4) cannot be met with in-place optimization (streaming aggregation, per-shard memoization with correct invalidation, selective field read), the PR author posts a short proposal in the bead notes: measured achievable numbers, proposed substrate from {axon, sqlite, postgres}, and migration cost. Implementation of that substrate requires explicit user approval before landing.\n\n6. **Completeness test.** A fixture seeds sessions that would not appear under the old bead-event path (open-bead sessions, `ddx agent run` sessions, benchmark sessions). The new resolver returns rows for each of them. A matching integration test on the old code path (kept as a golden-output comparison for one cycle) asserts the new output is a strict superset in the cases it should be.\n\n7. **Freshness.** A session written via the sessions index writer (from `ddx-2ceb02fa`) is visible in the Efficacy query within 2s. Integration test writes a row and queries the aggregation.\n\n8. **Playwright smoke.** `/efficacy` opens, renders the rollup table, and clicks into `EfficacyAttempts` for one row — all within normal navigation budgets. Uses the 10k-row fixture; asserts the table has ≥5 rows rendered.\n\n9. **Cross-reference.** This bead is a dependent of `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index must exist). If `ddx-2ceb02fa` is not yet landed when this work starts, bead notes record the stub / interim source chosen and the timeline for switching over.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"d4874cfd75778a35969c330285e33a126e359108","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:47:04.443214848Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-0a33bc5f","depends_on_id":"ddx-2ceb02fa","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-23T01:21:53Z"}],"description":"## Observed\n\nThe Efficacy page at `/nodes/.../projects/.../efficacy` loads very slowly. User feedback: efficacy should really be \"an aggregation of sessions grouped by provider / harness / model\" — which is not what the current implementation does.\n\n## Ground truth (current implementation)\n\n`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:676-833` —\n\n- `EfficacyRows` builds its rollup from **bead evidence events** (`kind:routing` and `kind:cost` on every closed bead), not from sessions.\n- Computation path: `store.ReadAll()` → for every closed bead, read its event file → filter for routing/cost → per-(harness, provider, model) aggregate.\n- There is a cache (`efficacyMemo`), keyed by a fingerprint that itself walks every event on every closed bead to compute event count + max timestamp. So cache validation costs almost as much as cache rebuild.\n- Cost shape: `O(closed_beads × events_per_bead)` on every request (cache miss) and even on cache hit the fingerprint walk happens.\n\n## Why this is wrong (per user feedback)\n\n- Efficacy is a sessions-view concept: \"how is harness/provider/model X doing?\" Sessions are the natural row. Bead events are a lossy, indirect proxy — they only exist for *closed* beads, they duplicate session state, and they force coupling between the efficacy feature and bead lifecycle.\n- The bead-event path misses: open beads with in-flight sessions, sessions that didn't close a bead, benchmark/quorum sessions, non-execute-bead invocations (e.g., `ddx agent run`).\n- The sessions feed is already being reworked into a sharded pointer index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`). Efficacy should read from that index — single source of truth.\n\n## Proposed direction\n\n### Part 1 — Reshape the data source\n\n- Rebuild `EfficacyRows` as an aggregation over `sessions.index` (the sharded index from `ddx-2ceb02fa`), not over bead events.\n- Grouping key: `(harness, provider, model)` — optionally filterable by project, bead, label, time range.\n- Metrics per row: attempts (count), successes (count + rate), median input/output tokens, median duration, median cost. Identical shape to today; only the source changes.\n- Shard awareness: time-range queries map to a shard set (same pattern as `ddx-2ceb02fa`); the default view reads current + previous shard.\n\n### Part 2 — Fixture + baseline (honor user's 1,000+ record floor)\n\n- Perf harness target: aggregate ≥**1,000 session rows** (≥ 10 distinct `(harness, provider, model)` groups) within the budget below.\n- Stretch fixture: 50,000 session rows across 24 monthly shards. Measures steady-state behavior as the index grows.\n- Baseline captured in the bead notes before any optimization.\n\n### Part 3 — Perf targets (first pass, in-place)\n\n- Default Efficacy view (all-time) on a 10k-session, 2-shard fixture: p95 ≤ 150ms in-process / ≤ 400ms HTTP.\n- Date-filtered view (last 30 days, 1 shard): p95 ≤ 60ms in-process / ≤ 200ms HTTP.\n- If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, memoize-per-shard, streaming aggregation) **cannot hit these numbers** on the 50k stretch fixture, **do not silently change substrate** — raise the decision with the user. Candidate backends named by the user: axon, sqlite, postgres. Bring a recommendation + rationale, not a fait accompli.\n\n### Part 4 — Drop the bead-event read path\n\n- After the rewrite, remove `efficacyFingerprint`, `buildEfficacySnapshot` event iteration, `attemptsFromEvidence`, and `efficacyMemo`. They become dead weight once the source moves.\n- Evidence events on beads remain (they're bead audit trail, not efficacy data); nothing in bead lifecycle changes.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- The `comparisons` / \"compare arms\" feature on this page (tracked separately — see companion bead).\n- Project-scope filtering UI (the backend support can be added now; the UI widget is a follow-up).\n- Cross-machine / federated efficacy rollups.","efficacy_baseline":"recorded","events_attachment":"ddx-0a33bc5f/events.jsonl","execute-loop-last-detail":"agent: provider error: provider request timeout: wall-clock 15m0s","execute-loop-last-status":"execution_failed","execute-loop-no-changes-count":1,"execute-loop-retry-after":"2026-04-23T13:19:44Z","feature":"FEAT-019","id":"ddx-0a33bc5f","issue_type":"task","labels":["feat-008","feat-010","feat-019","efficacy","perf"],"notes":"Efficacy now depends on ddx-2ceb02fa. .ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:92:{"acceptance":"File library/personas/test-engineer.md exists with valid YAML frontmatter. Body covers the core stance: favor real e2e tests (no mocks at e2e layer); stubs over mocks at unit layer; contract tests between stub and real backend; zero mocks in integration tests; behavior over implementation; deterministic factories; behavior-naming; testing pyramid as shape not ratios; performance measurements require baseline + explicit boundary + reproducible harness. grep -E \"cli/internal/|docs/helix/|orchTestGitOps|fakeExecuteBeadGit|gateTestGitOps|mockExecutor|script\\.go|integration_helper_test\" library/personas/test-engineer.md returns no matches. No hard numeric coverage threshold presented as a universal rule. No \"TDD is the only valid approach\" framing. No retry-loop-around-flaky-test as acceptable pattern. No performance claim pattern without baseline + boundary. Sources section cites at least two external works.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-17T22:37:54Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1512290","created_at":"2026-04-17T22:10:38.363323602Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-13ff86c6","depends_on_id":"ddx-5241bb19","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-17T22:12:23Z"}],"description":"The existing library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md is the strongest shipped persona content-wise, but it references DDx-internal paths that only exist in the DDx repo (docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md, cli/internal/agent/script.go, named DDx legacy offenders like orchTestGitOps/fakeExecuteBeadGit/gateTestGitOps/mockExecutor).\n\nRename to library/personas/test-engineer.md and generalize.\n\n**CORE STANCES (load-bearing — preserve these):**\n- Favor real e2e tests. At the e2e layer, do not mock anything — real infrastructure, real processes, real network where applicable. E2e tests that mock the database or the HTTP layer are unit tests lying about their scope.\n- Stubs over mocks at the unit layer. Extract interfaces at domain boundaries; write in-memory stubs that return canned values set up by the test. Stubs do not record call sequences.\n- Contract tests between in-memory stubs and real backends — same suite runs against both, proving they are interchangeable. This is the discipline that keeps stubs honest.\n- Zero mocks in integration tests. Use real git (temp dir with scrubbed env), real filesystem (t.TempDir), real database (temp file / temp container), real HTTP (local test server). The only approved fake is at the AI-provider (or equivalent third-party-SDK) boundary.\n- Behavior over implementation — assert on observable state, not on call counts or invocation sequences.\n- Deterministic factory functions / fakers over static JSON fixtures.\n- Test names describe behavior (\"creates invoice when all line items valid\"), not positions (\"test_create_invoice_3\").\n- Testing pyramid as a *shape guide* (more unit than integration than e2e), not as mandated ratios.\n- **Performance measurements require a baseline and clear boundaries.** Any claim that something is \"fast\", \"scales\", or \"improved performance\" must include: (a) a numeric baseline to compare against (prior version, prior run, published SLO), (b) an explicit boundary — what unit of work is being measured, what environment, what is excluded (cold-start, warmup, network latency, etc.), (c) reproducible harness so the measurement can be re-run. \"It feels fast\" and \"benchmarks look good\" are not performance claims.\n\n**STRIP (cargo-cult testing dogma — do NOT ship):**\n- Hard coverage percentages as universal rules (\"\u003e= 80% overall\", \"100% on critical paths\"). Replace with: \"measure coverage where the project tracks it; coverage is a signal, not a target\".\n- Specific timing budgets as universal rules (\"unit \u003c 10ms\", \"integration \u003c 100ms\"). Replace with: \"tests should be fast enough that developers run them; the specific threshold is a project concern\".\n- Specific pyramid percentages (\"60% unit / 25% integration / 10% contract / 5% e2e\") — keep the shape, drop the numbers.\n- TDD as the only valid approach. Test-first is one workflow; test-alongside is another; test-after for a specific reason can be defensible. Do not call non-TDD \"worst practice\".\n- Tests-for-the-sake-of-tests (mirror-of-spec tests that add no signal, trivial getters/setters).\n- Mutation testing as a quality requirement. Mention as an optional signal, not a bar to clear.\n- Snapshot tests without a maintenance plan.\n- Retry loops around flaky tests as \"improvement\" — a retry loop hides a bug; fix the flake.\n- Branch coverage vs line coverage dogma — say \"coverage metrics matter less than whether tests catch real bugs\".\n\n**STRIP (DDx-internal specifics):**\n- References to cli/internal/agent/script.go (replace with generic \"a deterministic script-like fake at the AI-provider boundary\")\n- Named DDx legacy offenders (orchTestGitOps, fakeExecuteBeadGit, gateTestGitOps, mockExecutor) — these belong in a helix-test-engineer plugin persona, not this generic base\n- Authoritative-sources frontmatter section citing docs/helix/concerns.md and HELIX concern.md\n- Specific paths like cli/internal/bead, cli/internal/agent\n\nGround in external canon:\n- Kent Beck - Test-Driven Development: By Example\n- Martin Fowler - \"Mocks Arent Stubs\" (canonical reference for why call-sequence assertions are a code smell)\n- Brendan Gregg - Systems Performance methodology (for the baseline + boundary stance on perf measurement)\n- Anthropic Prompt Library (if a testing entry exists)\n- TDD Guard from anthropics/skills repo (if the automated-test-first-enforcement pattern is worth mentioning)\n\nBody structure: Philosophy / Approach / Anti-patterns / Sources.\n\nFrontmatter:\n---\nname: test-engineer\nroles: [test-engineer, quality-analyst]\ndescription: Testing specialist enforcing stubs-over-mocks, contract tests, real e2e, behavior-over-implementation, testing pyramid as shape not ratios, and baselined performance measurement\ntags: [testing, quality, performance]\n---\n\nIn-scope: library/personas/test-engineer.md (new)\nOut-of-scope: deletion of test-engineer-tdd.md (deprecation bead); helix-test-engineer plugin persona (HELIX concern)","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-17T22:37:54.294846139Z","id":"ddx-13ff86c6","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:implementation","area:personas"],"owner":"erik","parent":"ddx-94c28517","priority":2,"spec-id":"FEAT-006","status":"closed","title":"Generalize test-engineer persona — strip DDx-internal path references","updated_at":"2026-04-17T22:39:32.555980009Z"}
.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:129:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As an operator, I want a single view that shows every place my agents can run — endpoint providers and subprocess harnesses alike — with status, recent token usage, and quota utilization. When I click into one, I see a 7-day trend so I can tell if I'm about to run out.\n\n**Acceptance criteria:**\n\n1. **Deliverable 1 — Unified view.**\n   - New `harnessStatuses` GraphQL resolver returns subprocess harnesses (claude, codex, gemini, agent) with: name, kind=`harness`, reachable, detail, lastCheckedAt, defaultForProfile.\n   - Existing `providerStatuses` shape gains `kind=\\\"endpoint\\\"` and `lastCheckedAt`.\n   - UI page loads within 500ms on a warm cache — it renders from last-known status **first**, then patches rows live as async probes return. Playwright asserts the table is interactive within 500ms of navigation and that row status patches within 5s of the probe completing.\n   - Subprocess harnesses claude and codex **appear in the table** on a machine where their binaries are installed, with correct reachable status. Integration test.\n\n2. **Deliverable 2 — Usage + quota fields (stubbable).**\n   - Per-row fields `usage` and `quota` as described. All fields nullable.\n   - For endpoint providers, `usage.tokensUsedLast24h` and `usage.tokensUsedLastHour` are computed from the sessions index (fixture: ≥1k session rows covering the last 24h). Unit test asserts correctness on a seeded window.\n   - For subprocess harnesses that expose rate-limit headers (claude, codex), parsed headers populate `quota.ceilingTokens`, `quota.ceilingWindowSeconds`, `quota.remaining`, `quota.resetAt`. Parser tested with captured header fixtures from each harness.\n   - For harnesses/providers that expose nothing, fields remain null and the UI renders `—` / \"not reported\"; no fabricated data.\n   - Utilization bar renders when both `usage` and `quota.ceilingTokens` are present. Sparkline renders when ≥6 hourly buckets of usage are available.\n\n3. **Deliverable 3 — Trend detail.**\n   - Detail route `/nodes/.../providers/[name]` shows 7-day and 30-day tokens/hour + requests/hour time series, with quota ceiling overlay when known.\n   - Buckets computed server-side from the sessions index; the client receives ≤ 200 data points per series.\n   - \"Projected to hit quota in ~X\" callout uses last-24h slope only. Renders only when `quota.ceilingTokens` is known and the last-24h slope is non-zero positive; otherwise absent.\n   - Playwright asserts the detail route renders with seeded usage and the projection text appears for a fixture that has a ceiling + ascending usage.\n\n4. **Perf posture.**\n   - Fixture for perf test: ≥1,000 session index rows covering the last 30 days with usage distributed across ≥5 endpoints/harnesses. Per standing directive in `ddx-9ce6842a`.\n   - Targets: unified list p95 ≤ 200ms HTTP (excluding async probes); trend detail p95 ≤ 400ms HTTP on the fixture.\n   - If in-place aggregation cannot hit the detail-view p95 on a 10,000-row stretch fixture, pause and raise the DB-substrate decision with the user per the standing directive — do not silently switch.\n\n5. **Design note.**\n   - One-pager in `docs/helix/02-design/` before implementation recording the page-rename decision, the row model (common fields, kind-specific fields), async-probe strategy, and the quota source per harness/provider. PR links to it.\n\n6. **No regressions.**\n   - `ddx agent providers`, `ddx agent doctor`, `ddx agent list`, `ddx agent check` CLI commands continue to work unchanged.\n   - `defaultRouteStatus` query remains.\n\n7. **Cross-references.**\n   - Depends on `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index) for usage aggregation. If that bead is not yet landed when this work starts, ship Deliverable 1 standalone with usage/quota stubbed as \"not available,\" and re-open the remaining deliverables when the index is ready.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"7ef7a4b1c517483485821f99aec728292bbe83a1","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:48:37.871527387Z","description":"## Observed\n\nThree related issues on `/nodes/.../providers`:\n\n1. **Loads slowly.** The page fires two GraphQL queries on mount — `providerStatuses` (which probes every configured provider for live connectivity) and `defaultRouteStatus` (which resolves the default route). Both are synchronous and serialized only by `Promise.all`. Each provider probe is a real network call.\n2. **Missing harnesses.** The pane shows API-endpoint providers (qwen, minimax, etc. — anything wired through the service with an endpoint). It does not show subprocess-invoked harnesses like `claude` (Claude Code) or `codex`. That is accurate to the current `providerStatuses` resolver (`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_providers.go:15-50`, which calls `svc.ListProviders`) but not what an operator wants: the operator thinks of \"where their agent work can run,\" which includes both endpoint providers AND subprocess harnesses.\n3. **No quota visibility.** Operator need: see current token utilization rate + quota utilization per harness/provider, trend it, and use the trend to decide whether to throttle before hitting a cap.\n\n## Root cause by area\n\n- **Slow load:** `svc.ListProviders` probes every configured provider for liveness. Probes are serial within the service call, and the whole query blocks page render.\n- **Missing harnesses:** Subprocess harnesses (claude, codex, gemini) are first-class in the CLI (`ddx agent list`, `ddx agent doctor`, `ddx agent check`) but are not surfaced via `providerStatuses`. They have their own status concepts — binary installed, credentials configured, rate-limit headroom — that don't map cleanly onto the endpoint-provider model.\n- **No quota:** No existing data source captures rolling token usage vs quota. Quota information varies wildly by harness: Claude Code has message / session headroom concepts from response headers; codex has rate-limit headers; endpoint providers have API quota per key. There is no unified representation today.\n\n## Proposed direction\n\nThree deliverables, sequenced from easy to hard.\n\n### Deliverable 1 — Unified \"Agent endpoints\" view (renames/rescopes the page)\n\n- Rename the surface from \"Providers\" to **\"Agent endpoints\"** (or similar — confirm copy in the design note). The domain object is \"a place where agent work can run,\" not \"an API provider.\"\n- Merge the data: `providerStatuses` stays as the source for endpoint providers. A new `harnessStatuses` resolver (read from `svc.ListHarnesses` / existing `ddx agent list` + `ddx agent doctor` machinery) surfaces subprocess harnesses. Page renders both in one table with a `kind` column (`endpoint` | `harness`) — or two tables if design prefers.\n- Status fields common to both: name, kind, reachable (bool), detail (text), last-checked-at, default-for-profile (set of profile names).\n- Asynchronous liveness. The page renders an instant first paint from cached status; liveness probes fire in background and patch rows as they return. No synchronous probe-wall on load.\n\n### Deliverable 2 — Token \u0026 quota fields\n\n- Extend the status payload (per row) with: `usage { tokensUsedLastHour, tokensUsedLast24h, requestsLastHour, requestsLast24h }` and `quota { ceilingTokens?, ceilingWindowSeconds?, remaining?, resetAt? }`. Any field may be null if the provider/harness doesn't expose it.\n- Data sources:\n  - **Endpoint providers:** aggregate from the sessions index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) filtered to the last 1h / 24h, grouped by provider. Quota ceilings come from any headers we capture during calls, plus optional static config.\n  - **Subprocess harnesses:** same sessions-index aggregation for usage. Quota — parse rate-limit headers from the harness output where available (Claude Code, codex both emit them). For harnesses that don't, leave quota null and render \"not reported.\"\n- Row gains two small inline displays: utilization bar (used / ceiling for the quota window) and a 7-day sparkline of tokens/hour.\n\n### Deliverable 3 — Trend view\n\n- Click into a row → detail page showing: 7-day and 30-day time-series of tokens/hour and requests/hour, with the quota ceiling overlaid. Aggregations come from the sessions index, bucketed server-side to avoid shipping raw rows.\n- Simple \"projected to hit quota in ~X hours/days\" callout computed from the slope of the last 24h — enough to answer \"are we on a trend to run out?\" without building a forecasting system.\n\n## Cross-bead dependencies\n\n- Depends on `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index) for any usage-rate data. If that bead isn't landed when this work starts, the usage/quota features stub to \"not available\" and ship purely with the unified-view rename + async liveness.\n- Fixture / perf guidance: per the standing directive recorded in `ddx-9ce6842a`, any aggregation here is tested against ≥1k session records. Sources section cites at least 3 external works, not just textbook pattern names. grep -E 'cli/internal/|docs/helix/' library/personas/architect.md returns no matches.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-17T22:41:46Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1513247","created_at":"2026-04-17T22:11:08.350470009Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-2bc4d02c","depends_on_id":"ddx-5241bb19","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-17T22:12:23Z"}],"description":"Replace library/personas/architect-systems.md with library/personas/architect.md. The existing file is a textbook summary of C4, ADR, microservices, CQRS, SAGA, event sourcing — a laundry list of patterns without opinionated voice. Rewrite with real stances.\n\nCore stances:\n- Simplicity beats sophistication — the right architecture is the simplest one that works for the real load, not the one that scales to 10x imagined load\n- Prefer monolith first — microservices have operational cost that only pays back at real scale\n- Event-driven only when async decoupling earns it — not by default\n- Data model beats clever caching — design the schema right, the cache falls out naturally\n- Write ADRs for decisions that will bite future maintainers; don't write ADRs for decisions with no real alternatives\n- Most 'architecture' failures are requirements failures or team-structure failures in disguise\n\nBody structure: Philosophy / Approach / Anti-patterns / Sources.\n\nAnti-patterns:\n- Designing for 10x scale before validating 1x\n- Microservices because 'teams should own services' without validated team boundaries\n- Event sourcing for audit because 'it's clean' — audit logs solve audit without event sourcing's operational tax\n- Abstracting over unclear requirements (YAGNI at the architecture layer)\n- Reaching for a pattern name before understanding the actual constraint\n\nSources:\n- Anthropic Prompt Library architecture prompts (if any)\n- Gregor Hohpe's architecture writings\n- Stefan Tilkov's monolith/microservices talks\n- Martin Fowler 'Monolith First' and similar\n- Reference to the C4 model (Simon Brown) and ADR (Michael Nygard) as conventions for output, not dogma\n\nFrontmatter:\n---\nname: architect\nroles: [architect, technical-lead]\ndescription: Opinionated systems architect — simplicity-first, monolith-first, decisions with ADRs when they bite maintainers\ntags: [architecture, design, simplicity]\n---\n\nIn-scope:\n- library/personas/architect.md (new)\n\nOut-of-scope:\n- Deleting architect-systems.md (deprecation bead)\n- Other persona rewrites (separate beads)","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-17T22:41:46.498027045Z","id":"ddx-2bc4d02c","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:implementation","area:personas"],"owner":"erik","parent":"ddx-94c28517","priority":2,"spec-id":"FEAT-006","status":"closed","title":"Rewrite architect persona with real opinions on when to reach for each pattern","updated_at":"2026-04-17T22:45:00.829035311Z"}
.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:161:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As an operator, I expect the Sessions page to reflect reality — if I ran `ddx work` today, today's sessions appear within seconds. And I expect the feed not to silently break again the next time the agent execution path is refactored.\n\n**Acceptance criteria:**\n\n1. **Session-index source of truth.** A single writer (invoked from `service.Execute` or a thin wrapper that every production agent-execution path calls) appends one line per completed agent invocation to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl`. Fields are pointer-only: id, projectID, beadID, harness, surface, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, exitCode, bundlePath (relative), nativeLogRef (relative). No prompt or response bodies embedded.\n\n2. **Single-call-site guarantee.** Unit test enumerates every production code path that executes an agent (execute-bead, execute-loop, `ddx agent run`, quorum, replay if still present) and asserts each appends exactly one index row. If a new path is added later without wiring this up, the test fails.\n\n3. **Visibility restored.** GraphQL `sessions` query reads `sessions.index.jsonl`. Running `ddx work` (or any agent-execution path) against a fixture project causes the new session to appear in a GraphQL query within 2 seconds of completion. E2E test demonstrates this.\n\n4. **Body lazy-load.** A new resolver `session(id: ID!) { prompt response stderr }` loads the heavy fields on demand from the execution bundle (`.ddx/executions/\u003cts\u003e-\u003chash\u003e/`) or the agent-log path, without reading every session in the index.\n\n5. **Legacy migration.** A one-shot command (e.g. `ddx agent log reindex`) seeds the index from the existing `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` (397 MB corpus on the current repo), preserving historical sessions. Integration test uses a fixture `sessions.jsonl` and asserts the index has one row per legacy entry with all pointer fields populated.\n\n6. **Deprecated-file behavior.** After migration, the legacy `sessions.jsonl` is not read by the server. `ddx agent usage` and related CLI commands (`cli/cmd/agent_usage.go:197,246,348`, `cli/cmd/status.go:179`) are updated to read from the index too, verified by their existing tests.\n\n7. **Perf sanity.** The GraphQL `sessions` list resolves in ≤100ms p95 on a fixture with 10,000 index entries; current behavior times out or OOMs. Benchmark captured in bead notes as baseline + after-fix.\n\n8. **UI note.** If there is a gap in the index (e.g. the 2026-04-19 → 2026-04-22 gap on the current repo, assuming backfill from executions/agent-logs is not feasible), the Sessions page surfaces a single-line banner \"No sessions recorded between X and Y\" so the gap is not silently invisible. If backfill is feasible, do it instead and skip the banner.\n\n9. **Playwright e2e.**\n   - Seeds a fixture project with `.ddx/executions/` bundles from both before and after a simulated gap.\n   - Runs the migration command.\n   - Opens `/sessions`, asserts the most recent session timestamp matches the latest bundle, asserts a clicked row lazy-loads prompt/response from the bundle rather than from an embedded payload.\n   - Asserts the feed updates live (within 2s) after a new simulated session is written.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"d733f7f20828708c5179f0ceff9462eaa34654eb","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:22:41.990684692Z","description":"## Observed\n\nThe Sessions page at `/nodes/.../projects/.../sessions` shows nothing newer than **2026-04-19**, three days ago. ddx is definitely still running — `.ddx/executions/` contains bundles from 2026-04-22 (today), `agent-s-*.jsonl` from 2026-04-21, `agent-loop-*.jsonl` from 2026-04-20. The UI simply cannot see them.\n\n## Root cause\n\nThe Sessions GraphQL resolver reads exclusively from `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` (`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:504-516`). That file stopped being appended on 2026-04-19 19:46.\n\nThe only writer is `Runner.logSession` (`cli/internal/agent/runner.go:847-914`). It is called from four sites today:\n\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:229` (Runner.Run — legacy)\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner_service.go:216`\n- `cli/internal/agent/claude_stream.go:516`\n- `cli/internal/agent/virtual.go:206`\n\nProduction execute-bead / execute-loop no longer goes through any of them. The Runner-to-service refactor series (`5b933103`, `280f58e0`, `74eceda9`, `7cc1eb58`, `e05c785e`, `9f79f037`) routed the common path through `service.Execute` directly and did not carry forward the sessions.jsonl append. The last sessions.jsonl write lines up with the day that refactor set became default.\n\n## Secondary issue (same fix should address)\n\n`sessions.jsonl` is 397 MB for only 895 entries because each entry embeds the full `result.Output` (see `SessionEntry.Response` at `runner.go:879`). Growth is unbounded, there is no rotation, and `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` (`state_graphql.go:476-490`) reads the whole file, parses every entry, and sorts in memory on every query. Even if we just restore the writer, the feed is fragile and will become a perf problem again quickly — relevant to `ddx-ad0db8fd` (bead detail was also O(N) scan).\n\n## Proposed direction\n\nDo not simply paper over by re-plumbing `logSession` into `service.Execute`. The right fix is:\n\n1. **Decide the source of truth for \"a session\".** Current candidates on disk:\n   - `.ddx/executions/\u003cts\u003e-\u003chash\u003e/{manifest.json,prompt.md,result.json}` — already written per execute-bead attempt.\n   - `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-*.jsonl` and `agent-s-*.jsonl` — per-run streams from the agent harness.\n   - Bead evidence events (kind:cost already written per attempt, see `execute_bead.go:492`).\n   A session record should be derivable from a combination of execution bundles + agent-log streams, not from a separately-appended aggregate. The aggregate should be an **index**, not a **copy**.\n\n2. **Introduce a lightweight index.** A thin `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl` containing `{id, projectID, beadID, harness, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, bundlePath}` — pointer fields only, no embedded response bodies. Existing `sessions.jsonl` is deprecated to a read-only legacy file during a grace period.\n\n3. **Write the index from `service.Execute`** (or a wrapper the execute-bead / run / execute-loop paths all share). One place to touch. Unit test guards that every production code path that executes an agent appends exactly one index row.\n\n4. **GraphQL reads the index, streams bodies on demand.** `sessions { edges { node { ... } } }` reads the index. A new `session(id: ID!) { prompt, response, stderr }` lazy-loads the heavy fields from the execution bundle or agent-log file when the user expands a row.\n\n5. **Legacy sessions.jsonl compatibility.** The index can be seeded once from the existing 397 MB file so historical data is not lost, then the old file is not read again. One-shot migration command (`ddx agent log reindex` or similar).\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Replaying or regenerating sessions for the 2026-04-19 → 2026-04-22 gap. If the evidence exists in executions/agent-logs we can backfill the index; if not, accept the gap and cap it with a dated marker in the UI.\n- A full telemetry rework. This bead just restores visibility and fixes the shape.","events_attachment":"ddx-2ceb02fa/events.jsonl","execute-loop-last-detail":"escalation exhausted: unsupported model \"minimax/minimax-m2.7\" for harness \"gemini\"; supported models: gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-flash-lite","execute-loop-last-status":"execution_failed","execute-loop-no-changes-count":1,"feature":"FEAT-010","id":"ddx-2ceb02fa","issue_type":"bug","labels":["feat-008","feat-010","feat-006","perf","observability"],"notes":"## 2026-04-22 — User-directed amendment: shard the index\n\nPer user feedback: a single `sessions.index.jsonl` just replays the same problem at smaller scale. The aggregate needs to be split.\n\n### Revised storage shape\n\n- Directory: `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/` (per-project, under each registered project's `.ddx`).\n- Inside: **monthly shards** named `sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`.\n- Each shard is pointer-only, same fields as the previously-proposed index (id, projectID, beadID, harness, surface, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, exitCode, bundlePath, nativeLogRef).\n- Writer resolves the target shard from `time.Now().UTC()` at append time and creates the shard file lazily on first write of the month.\n- Heavy bodies (prompt, response, stderr) stay where they already live — `.ddx/executions/\u003cts\u003e-\u003chash\u003e/` and agent-log streams — and are lazy-loaded by the `session(id:)` resolver.\n\n### Why monthly, not daily / size-based / SQLite\n\n- **Daily** — produces tens of thousands of files over the project lifetime; ReadDir becomes the cost.\n- **Size-based** — requires coordinating rotation with the append; race-prone and hard to test.\n- **SQLite** — overkill for first pass; revisit if a single monthly shard grows past tens of MB.\n- **Monthly** — ≤12 shards/year, human-readable filenames, date-range queries map trivially to shard selection, bounded shard size in practice.\n\n### Reader behavior\n\n- Default Sessions query loads the **current + previous shard** (covers \"recent activity\" for any current date).\n- Date-range queries map the range to a shard set and read only those.\n- Pagination beyond the default window triggers older-shard loads on demand.\n- No shard-coordination lock needed: each month's file is append-only and only the current-month shard is actively written.\n\n### Legacy migration\n\n- `ddx agent log reindex` reads the existing `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` once and splits by each entry's `timestamp` field into the appropriate monthly shards under `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/`.\n- After migration, the legacy file is renamed `sessions.jsonl.legacy` (not deleted) and never read again by the server. Hand-removal is the user's call.\n- Idempotent: re-running the command merges without duplicating (index row dedup by `id`).\n\n### Revised acceptance criteria (supersedes criteria 1, 5, 6, 7 on original description)\n\n1. **Sharded index writer.** Single shared call site (from `service.Execute` or equivalent wrapper) appends one line to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, computing the shard from `time.Now().UTC()`. Test: write across a simulated month boundary and assert two shards exist with the correct split.\n\n5. **Legacy migration shards too.** `ddx agent log reindex` splits `sessions.jsonl` into monthly shards by entry timestamp. Integration test uses a fixture with entries across 3 distinct months and asserts 3 shards are produced with correct contents and no duplicates on re-run.\n\n6. **Server reads the shard directory.** `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` reads `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/*.jsonl`, respects any date-range arg, and loads only the shards that intersect the range. Unit test seeds 6 monthly shards and asserts a 2-month query reads exactly 2 files.\n\n7. **Perf on sharded index.** Default Sessions view (current + previous shard) resolves in ≤50ms p95 on a fixture with 12 monthly shards × 10k entries each (120k total). Capture baseline (single-file read of same corpus) vs sharded in bead notes.\n\n(Criteria 2, 3, 4, 8, 9 from the original acceptance list remain as written.)\n\n### Scope clarifier\n\nThis amendment does not change the single-call-site writer guarantee or the lazy-body resolver — those remain the load-bearing ideas. The amendment is specifically about **how the pointer index is physically stored**.\n## 2026-04-23 — Implementation benchmark and evidence\n\nBaseline: `go test ./internal/agent -run '^$' -bench 'Benchmark(ReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow|LegacySessionsJSONLFullScan)$' -benchtime=1x` measured legacy single-file full scan/sort of 120k legacy-shaped rows with 2KB embedded responses at `BenchmarkLegacySessionsJSONLFullScan-12 1 1731705957 ns/op`.\n\nAfter fix: the same command measured the sharded default-window reader at `BenchmarkReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow-12 1 8103661 ns/op` for 12 monthly shards x 10k rows each (120k total), with the default query reading only the current + previous shard window. Environment footer: linux/arm64, Go go1.26.2.\n\nAcceptance evidence: `TestSessionIndexWritesMonthlyShards`, `TestSessionIndexShardFilesSelectsOnlyIntersectingDateRange`, `TestReindexLegacySessionsSplitsShardsAndDedupes`, `TestRunViaServiceWithAppendsOneSessionIndexRow`, `TestProductionAgentExecutionPathsUseIndexedServiceWriter`, `TestGraphQLAgentSessions`, `TestAgentLogReindexMigratesLegacyFile`, existing agent usage/status/processmetrics tests, and `e2e/sessions.spec.ts` cover writer sharding, bounded reads, migration, lazy body loading, deprecated-file read removal, UI gap banner, and live refresh.\n\nREVIEW:BLOCK\n\nMissing session index writer: no changes to `service.Execute`, shared execution wrappers, or agent execution paths to append `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl`.\nMissing GraphQL changes: no update to `cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go` for index-backed `sessions` or lazy `session(id:)` body loading.\nMissing migration command: no `ddx agent log reindex` or equivalent CLI implementation and no fixture-based migration test.\nMissing CLI usage/status updates: no changes to `cli/cmd/agent_usage.go` or `cli/cmd/status.go` to stop reading legacy `sessions.jsonl`.\nMissing performance validation: no benchmark or fixture proving 10,000 index entries resolve within the required threshold.\nMissing UI gap/banner behavior and Playwright coverage for `/sessions`, lazy loading, migration, and live feed updates.\n\nREVIEW:BLOCK\n\n**AC 1–3**: No `sessions.index.jsonl` schema, writer in `service.Execute`, or updated GraphQL resolver in `cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go`.\n**AC 4**: No `session(id: ID!)` resolver for lazy-loading prompt/response/stderr from execution bundles.\n**AC 5**: No `ddx agent log reindex` command or migration logic.\n**AC 6**: `cli/cmd/agent_usage.go` and `cli/cmd/status.go` are untouched.\n**AC 7**: No benchmark captured.\n**AC 8–9**: No frontend changes or Playwright tests.\n\nREVIEW:BLOCK\n\nMissing `service.Execute` or shared agent-execution wrapper change to append one pointer-only row to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl`.\nMissing unit test proving execute-bead, execute-loop, `ddx agent run`, quorum, and replay paths append exactly one index row.\nMissing `cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go` update so GraphQL `sessions` reads `sessions.index.jsonl`.\nMissing new GraphQL `session(id: ID!)` resolver for lazy-loading prompt, response, and stderr from execution bundles or native logs.\nMissing `ddx agent log reindex` or equivalent migration command, plus integration test for legacy `sessions.jsonl`.\nMissing updates to `cli/cmd/agent_usage.go` and `cli/cmd/status.go` to stop reading legacy `sessions.jsonl`.\nMissing 10,000-entry GraphQL sessions benchmark and recorded baseline/after-fix bead notes.\nMissing Sessions UI gap banner or feasible Philosophy section names YAGNI, KISS, and DOWITYTD by name and includes the \"performance claims require baselines\" stance and the \"favor real e2e over adding mocks\" stance. Anti-patterns section lists at least 5 concrete patterns (speculative abstraction, unrelated refactor during bug fix, error handling for impossible cases, comments restating code, performance claims without baseline). Sources section cites at least two external works. grep -E \"cli/internal/|docs/helix/\" library/personas/implementer.md returns no matches.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-17T22:41:46Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1513225","created_at":"2026-04-17T22:10:54.294309117Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-2da7da89","depends_on_id":"ddx-5241bb19","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-17T22:12:23Z"}],"description":"Replace library/personas/pragmatic-implementer.md with library/personas/implementer.md. The existing file has a generic tutorial tone; rewrite with real opinions.\n\nCore stances:\n- YAGNI — implement only what is specified; no speculative features or \"while we are here\" additions\n- KISS — simplest solution that meets requirements; avoid clever code and premature optimization\n- DOWITYTD (Do Only What I Told You To Do) — stop when the task is complete\n- Ship tests with code — implementations without a test command referenced in AC are half-done (but: test-first vs test-alongside vs test-after is a workflow choice, not mandated by this persona — see the test-engineer persona for the testing stance; this persona requires tests exist and pass, not a specific methodology)\n- Do not design for hypothetical future requirements. Three similar lines is better than a premature abstraction.\n- **Performance claims require baselines.** Do not add code \"for performance\" without a baseline measurement and a clear boundary describing what is being measured. \"This is faster\" without a number is not a justification. (Cross-reference test-engineer for the measurement discipline.)\n- **Favor real e2e validation at the integration boundary.** When in doubt between a layer of abstraction/mocking and writing a real integration test, write the real integration test. The cost of mocks compounds; the cost of a real e2e test is paid once.\n\nBody structure: Philosophy / Approach / Anti-patterns / Sources.\n\nAnti-patterns to call out:\n- Speculative abstraction (base classes, interfaces, config knobs for \"someday\")\n- Refactoring unrelated code during a bug fix (\"while we are here...\")\n- Adding error handling for scenarios that cannot happen (trust internal invariants; validate at system boundaries)\n- Leaving TODO comments for problems that should block the current change\n- Comments that restate what the code does (well-named identifiers already do that)\n- Adding a mock or stub when a real integration test would be simpler and catch more bugs\n- Making performance claims without baselines or boundaries\n\nSources:\n- YAGNI principle (c2.com wiki, various Martin Fowler articles)\n- Anthropic Prompt Library entries for coding agents, if any fit\n- Relevant DDx system-prompt content from library/prompts/claude/system-prompts/focused.md (already documents YAGNI/KISS/DOWITYTD)\n- Brendan Gregg on performance measurement discipline (baseline + boundary)\n\nFrontmatter:\n---\nname: implementer\nroles: [implementer, software-engineer]\ndescription: Ships the specified scope, nothing more — YAGNI/KISS/DOWITYTD with tests included, real e2e favored over mocks, performance claims only with baselines\ntags: [implementation, shipping, minimalism]\n---\n\nIn-scope: library/personas/implementer.md (new)\nOut-of-scope: Deleting pragmatic-implementer.md (deprecation bead); other persona rewrites (separate beads)","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-17T22:41:46.437282625Z","id":"ddx-2da7da89","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:implementation","area:personas"],"owner":"erik","parent":"ddx-94c28517","priority":2,"spec-id":"FEAT-006","status":"closed","title":"Rewrite implementer persona with opinionated YAGNI/KISS stance","updated_at":"2026-04-17T22:45:00.750092403Z"}
.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:182:{"archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-29T17:52:21Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"932496","closing_commit_sha":"0276dfa112695a0d8541d33c42fc18afd4fb2317","created_at":"2026-04-29T15:20:29.53796814Z","description":"Per plan: docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-29-artifact-and-run-architecture.md.\n\nScope of vision update:\n\n(0) PREAMBLE for Core Thesis (canonical wording — use verbatim):\n\n\u003e Software is leverage. Fifty years of practice has shown that there are physics of software that must be respected to produce and maintain systems at scale. The agentic era allows every team to produce systems at scale and makes this even more important.\n\u003e\n\u003e Generative AI brings its own physics. DDx exists at the seam where these two meet — the fulcrum that lets software's lever do work. Without it, shipping software with agents quietly degrades into prompt-and-pray. The six principles below are the load-bearing claims on both sides.\n\nThe preamble REPLACES the existing two-paragraph Core Thesis. Existing paragraph 2 (\"Creating documentation and using it as an abstraction... DDx encodes that insight into infrastructure...\") drifts into positioning rather than thesis — its substance is captured by principle #1 plus existing 'What DDx Is' section. Drop it from Core Thesis when this bead lands.\n\n(1) Thesis principles — restructure Core Thesis around 6 principles in 3 groups (Physics of Software / Physics of Generative AI / The intersection — DDx's reason to exist). Final formulations:\n\nA. Physics of Software (engineering truths agents amplify):\n1. Abstraction is the lever. Multi-level artifact stacks (vision -\u003e spec -\u003e test -\u003e code) with maintained relationships are how intent propagates without being lost. True for human teams; load-bearing for agents because they don't carry tacit knowledge between invocations.\n2. Software is iteration over tracked work. Repeated trials over an explicit work substrate — beads, queues, dependency DAGs — is how software gets built. Pre-existed agents; agents make the substrate non-optional.\n3. Methodology is plural. Different teams, projects, and problem domains demand different workflows — waterfall, agile, kanban, HELIX, ad-hoc. No tool that bakes one in survives the rest. DDx provides primitives (artifacts, runs, beads) that any methodology composes.\n\nB. Physics of Generative AI (claims about LLM behavior):\n4. LLMs are stochastic, unreliable, and costly. Cost-tier ladders, ensemble verification, and 'cheapest model that works' are the operating shape of agentic work, not optimizations.\n5. Evidence provides memory. Agents carry no state between invocations and outputs aren't bit-reproducible. The only thing that survives a run is what we captured as it happened. That captured evidence is the substrate for evaluation, trust, debugging, and learning — without it, every other principle degrades to anecdote.\n\nC. The intersection — DDx's reason to exist:\n6. Human-AI collaboration is the fulcrum. Abstraction levers intent across the artifact stack, but only collaboration converts leverage into shipped software. Humans supply intent and accountability; AI supplies volume and execution. DDx is the toolkit at the seam — handoffs in both directions, at every level.\n\nNote: #1 and #6 form a deliberate rhetorical bookend (lever + fulcrum = leverage). The preamble primes the metaphor without spoiling it.\n\n(2) Operating principles — keep existing list (Git-native, file-first, etc.) but add one-line preamble: 'Operating principles are the choices DDx makes in response to the physics above.' Update Principle #1 from 'Documents are the product' to 'Artifacts are the product (documents primary, other media supported).'\n\n(3) Artifact + 3-layer architecture changes (per original bead scope):\n- Thesis copy: 'documents AI agents consume' -\u003e 'artifacts agents produce and consume'\n- Artifact-management bullet: broaden to non-document media + generators\n- One sentence acknowledging four-way producer/consumer space (no separate matrix subsection)\n- New Design-Philosophy subsection: Three-layer run architecture (ddx run / ddx try / ddx work)\n- KVP table additions: 'Multi-media artifact graph'; 'Three-layer run architecture (run/try/work)'; note invocation is upstream\n\nDo NOT touch docs/helix/01-frame/principles.md — that's HELIX engineering decision-guide, separate concern.","events_attachment":"ddx-33e07890/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-29T17:54:21.834769995Z","id":"ddx-33e07890","issue_type":"task","labels":["frame","plan-2026-04-29"],"owner":"erik","priority":1,"session_id":"eb-b3b088c2","status":"closed","title":"[artifact-run-arch] update product-vision.md for artifact + 3-layer architecture","updated_at":"2026-04-29T17:55:16.22235812Z"}

.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:284:{"acceptance":"AC1. ResolveTierModelRef (or its replacement) consults the model catalog as the default source of tier→model mapping when agent.routing.model_overrides is not set, returning the first surface→model pair whose harness is available and whose model resolves to a healthy provider.\n\nAC2. `ddx agent execute-loop --once --local --no-adaptive-min-tier` succeeds against a fresh project (no model_overrides in .ddx/config.yaml) when the catalog has at least one tier with a reachable harness/model/provider chain.\n\nAC3. When EVERY catalog surface for the resolved tier is unreachable, the tier-attempt event records the concrete reason for each surface tried (e.g. 'codex: harness not installed', 'claude: provider cooldown', 'embedded-openai: model qwen3.6 has no reachable provider'), not a generic 'no viable harness found' with empty fields.\n\nAC4. `ddx agent doctor` (or `ddx agent catalog show`) emits a warning when a tier's catalog entries reference a model id that has no matching named route AND no provider /v1/models entry across configured providers, so the user discovers the gap before invoking execute-loop.\n\nAC5. Per-project agent.routing.model_overrides remains a supported override that wins over catalog defaults — backwards compatible.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-29T04:14:56Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"131811","closing_commit_sha":"351dc52260be4f5d45484fc8a8d77500fb35f48b","created_at":"2026-04-29T03:28:05.434417294Z","description":"When agent.routing.model_overrides is not set in a project's .ddx/config.yaml, ResolveTierModelRef in cli/internal/agent/profile_ladder.go:44 returns the literal tier name (\"cheap\", \"standard\", \"smart\") as the model identifier. ResolveRoute is then called with model=\"cheap\"/\"standard\"/\"smart\" — strings that match no model id and no named route. Every tier resolves to an empty harness/empty model and is recorded as a tier-attempt with body \"no viable harness found\". The loop exhausts all tiers and reports \"execute-loop: all tiers exhausted — no viable provider found\".\n\nThis is happening today on a workstation that has bragi/vidar/hel hosting qwen3.6, plus working claude and codex harnesses. execute-bead succeeds with the same providers when --harness/--model are pinned explicitly, proving the underlying routing is fine. Only the tier→model resolution path is broken.\n\nThe model catalog (~/.ddx/model-catalog.yaml or built-in defaults, surfaced via `ddx agent catalog show`) ALREADY contains the tier→surface→model mapping that would make this work. The resolver just doesn't consume it. Surfaces and concrete models are listed per tier (e.g. cheap: embedded-openai→qwen3.5-27b, codex→gpt-5.4-mini, claude→claude-haiku-4-5) — the resolver should walk these surfaces in order, picking the first whose harness is healthy and whose model resolves to a reachable provider via model_routes. Per-project model_overrides should be an OPTIONAL override layered on top of catalog defaults, not the only thing that makes tier resolution work.\n\nCaller-side workaround: every project must add agent.routing.model_overrides to .ddx/config.yaml before execute-loop will function. This is silent: there is no diagnostic from `ddx agent doctor`, no warning from `ddx agent catalog show`, and no hint in the \"no viable provider\" error pointing at the missing config. Users have to read source to understand why a fully-healthy provider/harness fleet produces zero viable tiers.\n\nRepro:\n1. Fresh project with .ddx/config.yaml lacking agent.routing.model_overrides\n2. Healthy provider config in ~/.config/agent/config.yaml (any qwen/claude/codex)\n3. `ddx agent execute-loop --once --local --no-adaptive-min-tier`\n4. Every tier-attempt event records \"no viable harness found\"; loop exits with 'all tiers exhausted'\n\nRelated symptom — the adaptive min-tier promotion uses cheap-tier success rate to skip cheap, but with the bug above no cheap-tier attempt has ever produced a successful harness invocation, so the trailing window is biased to 0% by design rather than by actual model performance.","events_attachment":"ddx-5538aa5b/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-29T04:40:26.540559641Z","id":"ddx-5538aa5b","issue_type":"bug","labels":["area:agent","area:routing","kind:design-flaw"],"owner":"ddx","priority":1,"session_id":"eb-f61475d0","status":"closed","title":"execute-loop: tier resolver should consume catalog instead of requiring per-project model_overrides","updated_at":"2026-04-29T04:41:11.568927737Z"}
