TestOMLXLiveSmoke(t *testing.T) {\\n+\\tbaseURL := os.Getenv(\\\"OMLX_SMOKE_URL\\\")\\n+\\tif baseURL == \\\"\\\" {\\n+\\t\\tt.Skip(\\\"OMLX_SMOKE_URL not set — live smoke requires an omlx endpoint\\\")\\n+\\t}\\n+\\tmodel := os.Getenv(\\\"OMLX_SMOKE_MODEL\\\")\\n+\\tif model == \\\"\\\" {\\n+\\t\\tt.Fatal(\\\"OMLX_SMOKE_MODEL is required when OMLX_SMOKE_URL is set\\\")\\n+\\t}\\n+\\n+\\tprovider := openai.New(openai.Config{\\n+\\t\\tBaseURL: baseURL,\\n+\\t\\tAPIKey:  os.Getenv(\\\"OMLX_SMOKE_API_KEY\\\"),\\n+\\t\\tModel:   model,\\n+\\t\\tFlavor:  \\\"omlx\\\",\\n+\\t})\\n+\\n+\\tr := NewRunner(Config{SessionLogDir: t.TempDir(), TimeoutMS: 120000})\\n+\\tr.LookPath = mockLookPath\\n+\\tr.AgentProvider = provider\\n+\\n+\\tresult, err := r.RunAgent(RunOptions{\\n+\\t\\tHarness: \\\"agent\\\",\\n+\\t\\tPrompt:  \\\"Reply with the single word 'ready' and nothing else.\\\",\\n+\\t\\tWorkDir: t.TempDir(),\\n+\\t})\\n+\\trequire.NoError(t, err)\\n+\\n+\\t// The regression signature is the precise error v0.3.13 surfaced when\\n+\\t// omlx emitted keep-alive SSE comment frames during reasoning warmup.\\n+\\t// Any bump that reintroduces it must be caught here before it ships.\\n+\\tassert.NotContains(t, result.Error, \\\"unexpected end of JSON input\\\",\\n+\\t\\t\\\"live omlx produced the v0.3.13 SSE comment-frame regression signature\\\")\\n+\\tassert.Equal(t, 0, result.ExitCode, \\\"live omlx smoke must close cleanly; error=%q\\\", result.Error)\\n+\\tassert.NotEmpty(t, result.Output, \\\"live omlx must return some content\\\")\\n+}\\ndiff --git a/cli/internal/agent/testdata/omlx-wire/README.md b/cli/internal/agent/testdata/omlx-wire/README.md\\nnew file mode 100644\\nindex 00000000..1f3cbdfa\\n--- /dev/null\\n+++ b/cli/internal/agent/testdata/omlx-wire/README.md\\n@@ -0,0 +1,61 @@\\n+# omlx SSE Wire Fixtures\\n+\\n+These fixtures drive `cli/internal/agent/omlx_e2e_test.go` — the consumer-side\\n+end-to-end smoke test that guards `ddx work` against regressions on the\\n+`ddx-agent → vidar-omlx` dispatch path. Context: bead **ddx-bbb2d177**, which\\n+was filed after v0.3.14 shipped the `sseCommentFilter` fix for bead\\n+**agent-f237e07b** (upstream).\\n+\\n+## File format\\n+\\n+Each fixture is a JSONL file with two record kinds:\\n+\\n+- First line: `{\\\"kind\\\":\\\"meta\\\", ...}` — describes the capture and the assertion\\n+  shape the replay test will enforce (expected status, substrings that must or\\n+  must not appear in the assembled content / error).\\n+- Remaining lines: `{\\\"kind\\\":\\\"frame\\\",\\\"body\\\":\\\"<raw SSE body>\\\"}` — one frame per\\n+  record, written verbatim (including `\\\\n\\\\n` terminators) to the test HTTP\\n+  response in arrival order.\\n+\\n+Frames include both `: keep-alive` SSE comment frames (the exact shape that\\n+triggered the v0.3.13 \\\"unexpected end of JSON input\\\" regression) and normal\\n+`data: {...}` chunks.\\n+\\n+## When to regenerate\\n+\\n+Regenerate fixtures only when real omlx server behavior changes in a way the\\n+existing captures no longer represent — for example, a new reasoning-phase\\n+frame shape, a header shift, or a chunk-boundary change. Do NOT regenerate\\n+just to refresh timestamps.\\n+\\n+## How to regenerate\\n+\\n+Use `scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh`, which wraps `ddx agent run` with\\n+`AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE_STREAM_FULL=1` (shipped in ddx-agent v0.3.14) so the full\\n+stream is captured to a sidecar JSONL, then reshapes it into the fixture\\n+format:\\n+\\n+```bash\\n+scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh \\\\\\n+  --endpoint http://vidar:1235/v1 \\\\\\n+  --model    Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit \\\\\\n+  --prompt   \\\"Reply with the word ready.\\\" \\\\\\n+  --name     happy-path \\\\\\n+  --status   success \\\\\\n+  --out      cli/internal/agent/testdata/omlx-wire/happy_path.jsonl\\n+```\\n+\\n+After the script writes the fixture, hand-edit the `meta.expected` block to\\n+pin the `content_contains` / `error_contains` / `finish_reason` assertions for\\n+the capture you just took — the script cannot infer those from the wire\\n+bytes alone.\\n+\\n+## Why fixtures live here instead of the upstream agent repo\\n+\\n+The agent repo (`DocumentDrivenDX/agent`) has its own provider-level SSE\\n+tests in `provider/openai/openai_test.go`. Those guard the SDK's handling of\\n+SSE framing in isolation. Extracts every `dir==\\\"response\\\"` body chunk in arrival order and emits\\n+#      one {\\\"kind\\\":\\\"frame\\\",\\\"body\\\":...} record per chunk.\\n+#   3. Regenerate only when the omlx server changes wire\\n+# behavior (new frame type, header shift, chunk-size change) in a way the\\n+# existing fixtures no longer represent.\\n+\\n+set -euo pipefail\\n+\\n+endpoint=\\\"\\\"\\n+model=\\\"\\\"\\n+prompt=\\\"\\\"\\n+out=\\\"\\\"\\n+name=\\\"\\\"\\n+status=\\\"success\\\"\\n+\\n+while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do\\n+  case \\\"$1\\\" in\\n+    --endpoint) endpoint=\\\"$2\\\"; shift 2 ;;\\n+    --model)    model=\\\"$2\\\"; shift 2 ;;\\n+    --prompt)   prompt=\\\"$2\\\"; shift 2 ;;\\n+    --out)      out=\\\"$2\\\"; shift 2 ;;\\n+    --name)     name=\\\"$2\\\"; shift 2 ;;\\n+    --status)   status=\\\"$2\\\"; shift 2 ;;  # success | error\\n+    -h|--help)\\n+      sed -n '2,28p' \\\"$0\\\"\\n+      exit 0\\n+      ;;\\n+    *) echo \\\"unknown arg: $1\\\" >&2; Fixtures are authored either by hand\\n+// (the checked-in happy/error paths) or regenerated by\\n+// scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh against a live omlx endpoint.\\n+type omlxFixture struct {\\n+\\tMeta   omlxFixtureMeta\\n+\\tFrames []string // raw SSE bodies, written verbatim in order\\n+}\\n+\\n+type omlxFixtureMeta struct {\\n+\\tName        string              `json:\\\"name\\\"`\\n+\\tDescription string              `json:\\\"description\\\"`\\n+\\tExpected    omlxFixtureExpected `json:\\\"expected\\\"`\\n+}\\n+\\n+type omlxFixtureExpected struct {\\n+\\t// Status is \\\"success\\\" or \\\"error\\\".\\n+\\tStatus string `json:\\\"status\\\"`\\n+\\t// ErrorMustNotContain is a substring that the stream error — if any — must\\n+\\t// not contain. This is the regression guard: \\\"unexpected end of JSON input\\\"\\n+\\t// is the exact signature of the v0.3.13 SSE comment-frame defect (bead\\n+\\t// agent-f237e07b) that v0.3.14's sseCommentFilter fixed.\\n+\\tErrorMustNotContain string `json:\\\"error_must_not_contain\\\"`\\n+\\t// ContentContains is a substring the assembled content must contain on the\\n+\\t// happy path.\\n+\\tContentContains string `json:\\\"content_contains\\\"`\\n+\\t// FinishReason is the expected finish reason on the happy path.\\n+\\tFinishReason string `json:\\\"finish_reason\\\"`\\n+\\t// ErrorContains is a substring the surfaced error must contain on the\\n+\\t// error path — asserts the error is propagated rather than swallowed.\\n+\\tErrorContains string `json:\\\"error_contains\\\"`\\n+}\\n+\\n+// loadOMLXFixture reads a JSONL fixture from testdata/omlx-wire/. The first\\n+// line is the {\\\"kind\\\":\\\"meta\\\", ...} record; remaining {\\\"kind\\\":\\\"frame\\\",\\\"body\\\":...}\\n+// lines are the SSE bodies to stream in order.\\n+func loadOMLXFixture(t *testing.T, path string) omlxFixture {\\n+\\tt.Helper()\\n+\\tf, err := os.Open(path) // #nosec G304 -- test fixtures are checked in.\\n+\\trequire.NoError(t, err, \\\"open fixture %s\\\", path)\\n+\\tt.Cleanup(func() { _ = f.Close() })\\n+\\n+\\tvar fix omlxFixture\\n+\\tscanner := bufio.NewScanner(f)\\n+\\t// Default 64KB scanner buffer is tight for long reasoning lines.\\n+\\tscanner.Buffer(make([]byte, 64*1024), 1024*1024)\\n+\\tfirst := true\\n+\\tfor scanner.Scan() {\\n+\\t\\tline := strings.TrimSpace(scanner.Text())\\n+\\t\\tif line == \\\"\\\" {\\n+\\t\\t\\tcontinue\\n+\\t\\t}\\n+\\t\\tvar record struct {\\n+\\t\\t\\tKind string          `json:\\\"kind\\\"`\\n+\\t\\t\\tBody string          `json:\\\"body\\\"`\\n+\\t\\t\\tRaw  json.RawMessage `json:\\\"-\\\"`\\n+\\t\\t}\\n+\\t\\tif err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(line), &record); err != nil {\\n+\\t\\t\\tt.Fatalf(\\\"parsing fixture line in %s: %v\\\", path, err)\\n+\\t\\t}\\n+\\t\\tswitch record.Kind {\\n+\\t\\tcase \\\"meta\\\":\\n+\\t\\t\\tif !first {\\n+\\t\\t\\t\\tt.Fatalf(\\\"fixture %s: meta record must be the first non-empty line\\\", path)\\n+\\t\\t\\t}\\n+\\t\\t\\tif err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(line), &fix.Meta); err != nil {\\n+\\t\\t\\t\\tt.Fatalf(\\\"parsing meta in %s: %v\\\", path, err)\\n+\\t\\t\\t}\\n+\\t\\tcase \\\"frame\\\":\\n+\\t\\t\\tfix.Frames = append(fix.Frames, record.Body)\\n+\\t\\tdefault:\\n+\\t\\t\\tt.Fatalf(\\\"fixture %s: unknown record kind %q\\\", path, record.Kind)\\n+\\t\\t}\\n+\\t\\tfirst = false\\n+\\t}\\n+\\trequire.NoError(t, scanner.Err(), \\\"scanning fixture %s\\\", path)\\n+\\trequire.NotEmpty(t, fix.Meta.Name, \\\"fixture %s: missing meta.name\\\", path)\\n+\\trequire.NotEmpty(t, fix.Frames, \\\"fixture %s: no frame records\\\", path)\\n+\\treturn fix\\n+}\\n+\\n+// newOMLXReplayServer stands up an httptest.Server that behaves like a vidar-omlx\\n+// /v1/chat/completions endpoint by replaying the fixture's frames verbatim,\\n+// with a short inter-frame flush so the consumer's SSE decoder sees per-chunk\\n+// arrivals rather than one batched blob.\\n+func newOMLXReplayServer(t *testing.T, frames []string) *httptest.Server {\\n+\\tt.Helper()\\n+\\tsrv := httptest.NewServer(http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {\\n+\\t\\t// Match omlx's advertised Content-Type exactly — the SSE comment-frame\\n+\\t\\t// middleware in provider/openai activates only when Content-Type starts\\n+\\t\\t// with text/event-stream.\\n+\\t\\tw.Header().Set(\\\"Content-Type\\\", \\\"text/event-stream; charset=utf-8\\\")\\n+\\t\\tw.Header().Set(\\\"Cache-Control\\\", \\\"no-cache\\\")\\n+\\t\\tw.Header().Set(\\\"Transfer-Encoding\\\", \\\"chunked\\\")\\n+\\t\\tflusher, _ := w.(http.Flusher)\\n+\\t\\tfor _, body := range frames {\\n+\\t\\t\\tif _, err := io.WriteString(w, body); err != nil {\\n+\\t\\t\\t\\treturn\\n+\\t\\t\\t}\\n+\\t\\t\\tif flusher != nil {\\n+\\t\\t\\t\\tflusher.Flush()\\n+\\t\\t\\t}\\n+\\t\\t\\t// Small delay to reproduce per-frame arrival; long enough that the\\n+\\t\\t\\t// decoder cannot batch frames, short enough that the test stays\\n+\\t\\t\\t// under a second total.\\n+\\t\\t\\ttime.Sleep(5 * time.Millisecond)\\n+\\t\\t}\\n+\\t}))\\n+\\tt.Cleanup(srv.Close)\\n+\\treturn srv\\n+}\\n+\\n+// TestOMLXFixtureReplay_ConsumerEndToEnd drives a full ddx work orchestration\\n+// path (Runner.RunAgent → agentlib.Run → openai.Provider.ChatStream) against an\\n+// httptest double that replays recorded omlx SSE wire traces. failures are reported via result.ExitCode / result.Error\\\")\\n+\\n+\\t\\t\\t// The regression signature is always disallowed — on BOTH paths.\\n+\\t\\t\\t// v0.3.13 would surface this even for well-formed streams when\\n+\\t\\t\\t// keep-alive comment frames were present.\\n+\\t\\t\\tif fix.Meta.Expected.ErrorMustNotContain != \\\"\\\" {\\n+\\t\\t\\t\\tassert.NotContains(t, result.Error, fix.Meta.Expected.ErrorMustNotContain,\\n+\\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\\"consumer surfaced the v0.3.13 SSE comment-frame regression signature — the sseCommentFilter in ddx-agent v0.3.14+ is missing or broken\\\")\\n+\\t\\t\\t}\\n+\\n+\\t\\t\\tswitch fix.Meta.Expected.Status {\\n+\\t\\t\\tcase \\\"success\\\":\\n+\\t\\t\\t\\tassert.Equal(t, 0, result.ExitCode, \\\"happy-path fixture must close cleanly; got error=%q\\\", result.Error)\\n+\\t\\t\\t\\tassert.Empty(t, result.Error, \\\"happy-path must produce no error\\\")\\n+\\t\\t\\t\\tif want := fix.Meta.Expected.ContentContains; want != \\\"\\\" {\\n+\\t\\t\\t\\t\\tassert.Contains(t, result.Output, want,\\n+\\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\\"happy-path must assemble content from post-keep-alive data frames\\\")\\n+\\t\\t\\t\\t}\\n+\\t\\t\\tcase \\\"error\\\":\\n+\\t\\t\\t\\tassert.Equal(t, 1, result.ExitCode, \\\"error-path fixture must report failure via ExitCode=1\\\")\\n+\\t\\t\\t\\tassert.NotEmpty(t, result.Error, \\\"error-path must surface a non-empty error\\\")\\n+\\t\\t\\t\\tif want := fix.Meta.Expected.ErrorContains; consumer-side\\n+end-to-end smoke test that guards `ddx work` against regressions on the\\n+`ddx-agent → vidar-omlx` dispatch path. Context: bead **ddx-bbb2d177**, which\\n+was filed after v0.3.14 shipped the `sseCommentFilter` fix for bead\\n+**agent-f237e07b** (upstream).\\n+\\n+## File format\\n+\\n+Each fixture is a JSONL file with two record kinds:\\n+\\n+- First line: `{\\\"kind\\\":\\\"meta\\\", ...}` — describes the capture and the assertion\\n+  shape the replay test will enforce (expected status, substrings that must or\\n+  must not appear in the assembled content / error).\\n+- Remaining lines: `{\\\"kind\\\":\\\"frame\\\",\\\"body\\\":\\\"<raw SSE body>\\\"}` — one frame per\\n+  record, written verbatim (including `\\\\n\\\\n` terminators) to the test HTTP\\n+  response in arrival order.\\n+\\n+Frames include both `: keep-alive` SSE comment frames (the exact shape that\\n+triggered the v0.3.13 \\\"unexpected end of JSON input\\\" regression) and normal\\n+`data: {...}` chunks.\\n+\\n+## When to regenerate\\n+\\n+Regenerate fixtures only when real omlx server behavior changes in a way the\\n+existing captures no longer represent — for example, a new reasoning-phase\\n+frame shape, a header shift, or a chunk-boundary change. Those guard the SDK's handling of\\n+SSE framing in isolation. The fixtures in *this* directory guard the DDx\\n+consumer — `ddx work` → `Runner.RunAgent` → `agentlib.Run` — against both\\n+SDK regressions (a bad ddx-agent bump) and DDx-side regressions (a header or\\n+request-shape change that trips omlx's parser). Resolution](SD-015-agent-routing-and-catalog-resolution.md) and [SD-023 Agent Routing Visibility](SD-023-agent-routing-visibility.md).\\n\\nSD-015 and SD-023 describe *intent* — what the routing layer should do across\\nfive routing modes. This document describes *reality* — how one concrete CLI\\ninvocation flows through every layer of the code today, with file:line\\ncitations. It exists so a newcomer debugging a routing bug can read one\\ndocument instead of reverse-engineering the flow from `routing.go`,\\n`discovery.go`, `agent_runner.go`, `runner.go`, and `agent_cmd.go`.\\n\\n**Author's invocation used throughout this trace:**\\n\\n```bash\\nddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx --model-ref qwen/qwen3.6\\n```\\n\\n**Citations are anchored to base revision `654b4cdcd9200fa8db6c38d6af9dad2acfbaa779` (2026-04-18).**\\nLine numbers drift as the code evolves — see the *CI citation check* section\\nat the end for how this document is kept in sync.\\n\\n## Current Gaps (as of 2026-04-18)\\n\\nEach gap links to its tracking bead. `Provider` field; `--provider` is silently dropped during normalization unless it names a registered harness. | `cli/internal/agent/types.go:337` (struct def); `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:305` (only consumed as harness alias) | [ddx-8610020e](#) — routing: add Provider field to RouteRequest |\\n| 2 | `RunOptions.Provider` is a parallel path consumed by `resolveHarness()` and normalization |\\n| 4 | `scoreCandidate` has no provider-affinity bonus: when two harnesses can serve the same discovered model, the scorer does not prefer the harness whose provider actually advertised the model. | `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:477` | [ddx-3c5ba7cc](#) — tier escalation must respect `--provider` affinity |\\n| 5 | Fuzzy-match tiebreak picks shortest-suffix, not highest-version. `qwen3` against `[qwen3.5-27b, qwen3.6-35b]` picks `qwen3.5`. | `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:100` | [ddx-0216b966](#) — discovery fuzzy match tiebreak |\\n| 6 | No pre-dispatch DiscoverProviderModels(workDir, timeout)\\n    Note over Disc: 30s process-wide cache\\n    Disc-->>CLI: DiscoveryResult{Providers[]}\\n    CLI->>Plan: BuildCandidatePlans(req, states)\\n    loop each harness\\n        Plan->>Plan: evaluateCandidate\\n        Plan->>Plan: rejectIfNotViable\\n    end\\n    Plan->>Fuzzy: FuzzyMatchModel (when uncataloged)\\n    Fuzzy-->>Plan: resolved model ID\\n Result\\n    RunAgent-->>User: Result (stdout + session log)\\n```\\n\\n## Layer 1 — CLI flag parse\\n\\n`ddx work` is a thin alias for `ddx agent execute-loop`. All flags are cloned\\nfrom the inner command.\\n\\n- `cli/cmd/work.go:10` — `newWorkCommand` constructs the alias.\\n- `cli/cmd/work.go:40` — flags are cloned verbatim from `newAgentExecuteLoopCommand`.\\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1319` — `--harness` flag declaration.\\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1320` — `--model` flag declaration.\\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1321` — `--provider` flag declaration.\\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1322` — `--model-ref` flag declaration.\\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1327` — `--local` flag declaration.\\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1338` — `runAgentExecuteLoop` entry point reads flags.\\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1342` through `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1357` — flag values\\n  are plucked into local variables (`harness`, `model`, `provider`, `modelRef`,\\n  `effort`, `local`).\\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1374` — `--local` branch takes the inline path; without\\n  it the loop submits to the running DDx server.\\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1384` — `ValidateForExecuteLoop(harness, model, provider, modelRef)`\\n  runs before any bead is claimed.\\n\\nThe flag values eventually reach `ExecuteBead`, which in turn builds\\n`RunOptions`:\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/types.go:62` — `RunOptions` struct definition.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:498` — `runner.Run(RunOptions{...})`\\n  constructs the options, including `Provider: opts.Provider` on line 504.\\n\\n**Observation:** `RouteFlags` (`cli/internal/agent/types.go:51`) has a `Provider` field; so does\\n`RunOptions` (`cli/internal/agent/types.go:74`). They are the raw CLI inputs. The gap is not here\\n— it is at the normalization boundary (Layer 2).\\n\\n## Layer 2 — Normalize flags into `RouteRequest`\\n\\nThe normalization boundary converts raw CLI flags into the minimal routing ask\\nthat the routing engine evaluates.\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:289` — `NormalizeRouteRequest(flags RouteFlags, cfg Config, catalog *Catalog) RouteRequest`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:294` — `RouteRequest{Effort, Permissions}`\\n  initial struct. Note the absence of `Provider`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:303` — `flags.Harness != \\\"\\\"` branch assigns\\n  `HarnessOverride` directly.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:305` — `flags.Provider != \\\"\\\"` branch: the\\n  provider flag is **only** consulted as a harness-alias lookup. If\\n  `--provider vidar-omlx` does not match any entry in `builtinHarnesses`, the\\n  value is silently discarded.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:318` — `flags.Model != \\\"\\\"` branch routes the\\n  model through `catalog.NormalizeModelRef` to produce either `ModelRef` (known\\n  in catalog) or `ModelPin` (exact, bypasses catalog policy).\\n- `cli/internal/agent/types.go:337` — `RouteRequest` struct definition. Fields:\\n  `Profile`, `ModelRef`, `ModelPin`, `Effort`, `Permissions`, `HarnessOverride`.\\n  **No `Provider` field — see Gap #1.**\\n\\n**Concrete outcome for our invocation:**\\n`--provider vidar-omlx` does not name a registered harness, so the provider\\nflag is dropped at `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:305`. `--model-ref qwen/qwen3.6` flows through\\n`NormalizeModelRef`; since it is not in the shared catalog, it becomes a\\n`ModelPin`. The resulting `RouteRequest` carries only\\n`{ModelPin: \\\"qwen/qwen3.6\\\"}`.\\n\\n## Layer 3 — Discovery probe\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:117` — `init()` sets the process-wide cache\\n  TTL to `30 * time.Second`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:124` — `DiscoverProviderModels(workDir, timeout)` entry.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:125` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:130` — cache hit returns the cached result if still fresh.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:141` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:155` — probes every configured provider via `providerstatus.Probe`; silently skips unreachable ones (partial failure is tolerated).\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:162` — cache write under mutex.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:170` — `InvalidateDiscoveryCache` is the only\\n  way to force a re-probe in tests.\\n\\nDiscovery is invoked on the routing hot-path only when a `ModelPin` is not in\\nthe catalog:\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:576` — in `ProbeAndBuildCandidatePlans`, the\\n  discovery probe fires when `req.ModelPin != \\\"\\\" && req.ModelRef == \\\"\\\" && r.Discovery == nil`.\\n\\nDiscovery is invoked a second time on the dispatch path:\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:521` — in `resolveNativeAgentProvider`,\\n  `r.Discovery.ProvidersForModel(model)` looks up which providers advertised\\n  the requested model.\\n\\n## Layer 4 — Candidate planning\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:27` — `BuildCandidatePlans(req, stateOverride)` entry.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:33` through `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:46` — iterates every registered harness. `TestOnly` harnesses (script, virtual) are skipped unless `req.HarnessOverride` names them explicitly — this is the guard that prevents `script` from leaking into standard-tier fallback chains (see ddx-869848ec in the comment on line 40).\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:47` — calls `evaluateCandidate` per harness.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:106` — `evaluateCandidate(name, harness, req, stateOverride)`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:396` — `rejectIfNotViable(plan, req)` runs the\\n  viability gate. Checks:\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:399` — `!s.Installed`.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:402` — `!s.Reachable`.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:405` — `!s.Authenticated`.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:408` — quota exhausted.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:411` — degraded.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:414` — policy violation.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:418` — harness override mismatch.\\n\\nNote: none of these check *capability* — context window, tool support, effort\\nlevel support. That is Gap #6.\\n\\n## Layer 5 — Fuzzy matching\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:66` — `FuzzyMatchModel(input)` entry.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:70` — delegates to `fuzzyMatchInPool` using\\n  `d.AllModels()` (a flat deduplicated pool across all providers — not per-provider).\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:76` — `fuzzyMatchInPool(input, pool)`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:78` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:82` — exact-match short-circuit.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:89` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:96` — prefix match: candidate `m` is considered if `len(m) > len(input) && m[:len(input)] == input`. **The comparison is case-sensitive and treats slashes literally (Gap #3).**\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:100` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:106` — tiebreak: shortest suffix wins; alphabetical on equal suffix length. **Not version-aware (Gap #5).**\\n\\n**Concrete outcome for our invocation:**\\nPool likely contains `Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit`. - Writer resolves the target shard from `time.Now().UTC()` at append time and creates the shard file lazily on first write of the month.
- Heavy bodies (prompt, response, stderr) stay where they already live — `.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/` and agent-log streams — and are lazy-loaded by the `session(id:)` resolver.

### Why monthly, not daily / size-based / SQLite

- **Daily** — produces tens of thousands of files over the project lifetime; ReadDir becomes the cost.
- **Size-based** — requires coordinating rotation with the append; race-prone and hard to test.
- **SQLite** — overkill for first pass; revisit if a single monthly shard grows past tens of MB.
- **Monthly** — ≤12 shards/year, human-readable filenames, date-range queries map trivially to shard selection, bounded shard size in practice.

### Reader behavior

- Default Sessions query loads the **current + previous shard** (covers "recent activity" for any current date).
- Date-range queries map the range to a shard set and read only those.
- Pagination beyond the default window triggers older-shard loads on demand.
- No shard-coordination lock needed: each month's file is append-only and only the current-month shard is actively written.

### Legacy migration

- `ddx agent log reindex` reads the existing `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` once and splits by each entry's `timestamp` field into the appropriate monthly shards under `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/`.
- After migration, the legacy file is renamed `sessions.jsonl.legacy` (not deleted) and never read again by the server. Hand-removal is the user's call.
- Idempotent: re-running the command merges without duplicating (index row dedup by `id`).

### Revised acceptance criteria (supersedes criteria 1, 5, 6, 7 on original description)

1. **Sharded index writer.** Single shared call site (from `service.Execute` or equivalent wrapper) appends one line to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, computing the shard from `time.Now().UTC()`. Test: write across a simulated month boundary and assert two shards exist with the correct split.

5. **Legacy migration shards too.** `ddx agent log reindex` splits `sessions.jsonl` into monthly shards by entry timestamp. Integration test uses a fixture with entries across 3 distinct months and asserts 3 shards are produced with correct contents and no duplicates on re-run.

6. **Server reads the shard directory.** `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` reads `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/*.jsonl`, respects any date-range arg, and loads only the shards that intersect the range. Unit test seeds 6 monthly shards and asserts a 2-month query reads exactly 2 files.

7. **Perf on sharded index.** Default Sessions view (current + previous shard) resolves in ≤50ms p95 on a fixture with 12 monthly shards × 10k entries each (120k total). Capture baseline (single-file read of same corpus) vs sharded in bead notes.

(Criteria 2, 3, 4, 8, 9 from the original acceptance list remain as written.)

### Scope clarifier

This amendment does not change the single-call-site writer guarantee or the lazy-body resolver — those remain the load-bearing ideas. The amendment is specifically about **how the pointer index is physically stored**.

.ddx/executions/20260423T002632-1f60f8fb/prompt.md:5:## Observed

Per-project `/beads` page is noticeably slow; the cross-project "all beads" view is substantially slower. Related: `ddx-ad0db8fd` already identified the single-bead-detail slowness caused by the same storage pattern.

## Root cause (same pattern, different call site)

`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_beads.go:22,40` —

- `Beads` (cross-project) and `BeadsByProject` (single-project) both call `r.State.GetBeadSnapshots(status, label, projectID, search)`.
- `GetBeadSnapshots` (`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:49-105`) iterates every registered project, constructs a `bead.Store` per project, calls `ReadAll()` (scan + JSONL parse of every bead file), filters in Go. Cost is `O(projects × beads_per_project)` on every call, independent of the filter/page size.
- All filtering (status, label, project, search) is Go-side, post-read. The page-size window is applied in `beadConnectionFromSnapshots` after the full corpus is already materialized.
- Cross-project "all beads" = same loop, just without the projectID filter. It is the worst case by design.

This is the same shape that's breaking the Sessions feed (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) and single-bead detail (`ddx-ad0db8fd`). A point fix here is insufficient — we need a measurement surface first so we stop relying on anecdote.

## Proposed direction

### Part 1 — Perf harness (foundational; enables everything else)

Introduce a reusable GraphQL perf harness under `cli/internal/server/perf/` or `cli/internal/server/graphql/bench_test.go`:

- Seeded fixture builder: generates N projects × M beads with configurable distribution of status/labels/dependencies. Reuses existing store primitives (no fake data layer).
- Go benchmark + scriptable HTTP runner (wrapper around an already-running server) reporting p50/p95/p99 per query shape.
- Targets covered on day one: `bead(id:)`, `beadsByProject(projectID, first, after, status, label, search)`, `beads(projectID=nil, first, after, status, label)`, `docGraph`, `sessions` (so the sessions shard work `ddx-2ceb02fa` inherits this harness). New query targets are added by writing a single table row.
- Thresholds are **recorded, not asserted**, in the first pass. CI surfaces a regression if a threshold is breached beyond a configured delta (e.g., +25% p95). Initial pass is "land the harness and record baseline"; a follow-up wires it into CI.

### Part 2 — Fix the scan pattern

Minimum viable, in priority order:

1. **Scope by project when the query is scoped.** `BeadsByProject` should call a new `GetBeadSnapshotsForProject(projectID, filters…)` that only opens that one project's store. Trivial change; eliminates the cross-project factor for the common case.
2. **Early filter pushdown.** `GetBeadSnapshots` should apply status/label/projectID filters at the file-parse boundary where cheap, not after materializing every bead. (Still reads every file — deeper fix below.)
3. **Lightweight in-memory index with invalidation.** After (1) and (2), if "all beads" or search is still slow, introduce an index keyed `{projectID, beadID}` with basic metadata (status, labels, title, updatedAt). Refresh on bead write + debounced filesystem watch. Full-body reads stay lazy — the index never holds bodies. This is the same storage discipline we adopted for sessions in `ddx-2ceb02fa`.

The harness from Part 1 is the gate for choosing between (2) and (3); don't commit to the index without numbers.

## Out of scope

- Full-text search infrastructure (tracked separately if needed).
- Changing the on-disk bead format.
- Cross-node federation performance. Current concern is a single server reading a single workspace.

2. Cross-repo tracking bead axon-82b6f7b2 — full DDx-on-axon adoption checklist (links to all required axon-side capabilities)

LOCKED DECISIONS (do not relitigate; bake into TD):
1. Deployment: separate axon-server, operator-managed. DDx does NOT spawn or supervise Axon.
2. Auth: localhost-only by default; ts-net for remote. Axon already supports ts-net per its serve.rs (Tailscale mode + LocalAPI whois + role tags). Reuses ADR-006.
3. Events storage: separate ddx_bead_events collection with event_of links (Option B). Two collections in Axon.
4. Offline posture: refuse all bead ops when Axon unreachable. No local cache, no WAL.
5. Wire transport: GraphQL only via genqlient-generated Go client. NOT gRPC. Subscriptions over WebSocket for live updates.

Cross-repo dependencies (tracked in axon-82b6f7b2):
- axon-05c1019d (P2): GraphQL pattern-query support — needed for single-query ddx bead ready/blocked. Day 1 ships with two-phase fallback.
- FEAT-013 secondary indexes (P1 Draft in axon): needed for status-field query performance at >1000 beads.
- FEAT-017 schema evolution (P1 Draft in axon): needed for clean DDx schema upgrades over time. Day 1 ships with DDx-side lazy migration on read.
- ts-net auth: ALREADY SHIPPED in axon. No coordination needed.
- GraphQL CRUD + subscriptions: ALREADY SHIPPED in axon.
- Multi-namespace/database: ALREADY SHIPPED.

Adoption gates (per axon-82b6f7b2):
- Alpha: requires axon-05c1019d + everything already-shipped. Acceptable to ship with table-scan perf.
- Beta: requires FEAT-013 (indexes) for >1000-bead performance.
- GA: requires FEAT-013 + FEAT-017 + axon-05c1019d all landed.

Defaults the TD can adopt without rechecking with operator (revisit only if implementation surfaces a problem):
- Schema versioning: DDx-side lazy migrate on read until FEAT-017 lands; switch to axon-native then.
- ddx bead ready Day 1: two-phase GraphQL query.
- Backup/DR: operator-managed via Axon's own backing-store DR.

.ddx/executions/20260423T005327-4722df5d/prompt.md:11:## 2026-04-22 — User-directed amendment: shard the index

Per user feedback: a single `sessions.index.jsonl` just replays the same problem at smaller scale. The aggregate needs to be split.

### Revised storage shape

- Directory: `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/` (per-project, under each registered project's `.ddx`).
- Inside: **monthly shards** named `sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`.
- Each shard is pointer-only, same fields as the previously-proposed index (id, projectID, beadID, harness, surface, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, exitCode, bundlePath, nativeLogRef).
- Writer resolves the target shard from `time.Now().UTC()` at append time and creates the shard file lazily on first write of the month.
- Heavy bodies (prompt, response, stderr) stay where they already live — `.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/` and agent-log streams — and are lazy-loaded by the `session(id:)` resolver.

### Why monthly, not daily / size-based / SQLite

- **Daily** — produces tens of thousands of files over the project lifetime; ReadDir becomes the cost.
- **Size-based** — requires coordinating rotation with the append; race-prone and hard to test.
- **SQLite** — overkill for first pass; revisit if a single monthly shard grows past tens of MB.
- **Monthly** — ≤12 shards/year, human-readable filenames, date-range queries map trivially to shard selection, bounded shard size in practice.

### Reader behavior

- Default Sessions query loads the **current + previous shard** (covers "recent activity" for any current date).
- Date-range queries map the range to a shard set and read only those.
- Pagination beyond the default window triggers older-shard loads on demand.
- No shard-coordination lock needed: each month's file is append-only and only the current-month shard is actively written.

### Legacy migration

- `ddx agent log reindex` reads the existing `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` once and splits by each entry's `timestamp` field into the appropriate monthly shards under `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/`.
- After migration, the legacy file is renamed `sessions.jsonl.legacy` (not deleted) and never read again by the server. Hand-removal is the user's call.
- Idempotent: re-running the command merges without duplicating (index row dedup by `id`).

### Revised acceptance criteria (supersedes criteria 1, 5, 6, 7 on original description)

1. **Sharded index writer.** Single shared call site (from `service.Execute` or equivalent wrapper) appends one line to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, computing the shard from `time.Now().UTC()`. Test: write across a simulated month boundary and assert two shards exist with the correct split.

5. **Legacy migration shards too.** `ddx agent log reindex` splits `sessions.jsonl` into monthly shards by entry timestamp. Integration test uses a fixture with entries across 3 distinct months and asserts 3 shards are produced with correct contents and no duplicates on re-run.

6. **Server reads the shard directory.** `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` reads `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/*.jsonl`, respects any date-range arg, and loads only the shards that intersect the range. Unit test seeds 6 monthly shards and asserts a 2-month query reads exactly 2 files.

7. **Perf on sharded index.** Default Sessions view (current + previous shard) resolves in ≤50ms p95 on a fixture with 12 monthly shards × 10k entries each (120k total). Capture baseline (single-file read of same corpus) vs sharded in bead notes.

(Criteria 2, 3, 4, 8, 9 from the original acceptance list remain as written.)

### Scope clarifier

This amendment does not change the single-call-site writer guarantee or the lazy-body resolver — those remain the load-bearing ideas. The amendment is specifically about **how the pointer index is physically stored**.
## 2026-04-23 — Implementation benchmark and evidence

Baseline: `go test ./internal/agent -run '^$' -bench 'Benchmark(ReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow|LegacySessionsJSONLFullScan)$' -benchtime=1x` measured legacy single-file full scan/sort of 120k legacy-shaped rows with 2KB embedded responses at `BenchmarkLegacySessionsJSONLFullScan-12 1 1731705957 ns/op`.

After fix: the same command measured the sharded default-window reader at `BenchmarkReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow-12 1 8103661 ns/op` for 12 monthly shards x 10k rows each (120k total), with the default query reading only the current + previous shard window. Environment footer: linux/arm64, Go go1.26.2.

Acceptance evidence: `TestSessionIndexWritesMonthlyShards`, `TestSessionIndexShardFilesSelectsOnlyIntersectingDateRange`, `TestReindexLegacySessionsSplitsShardsAndDedupes`, `TestRunViaServiceWithAppendsOneSessionIndexRow`, `TestProductionAgentExecutionPathsUseIndexedServiceWriter`, `TestGraphQLAgentSessions`, `TestAgentLogReindexMigratesLegacyFile`, existing agent usage/status/processmetrics tests, and `e2e/sessions.spec.ts` cover writer sharding, bounded reads, migration, lazy body loading, deprecated-file read removal, UI gap banner, and live refresh.

REVIEW:BLOCK

Missing session index writer: no changes to `service.Execute`, shared execution wrappers, or agent execution paths to append `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl`.
Missing GraphQL changes: no update to `cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go` for index-backed `sessions` or lazy `session(id:)` body loading.
Missing migration command: no `ddx agent log reindex` or equivalent CLI implementation and no fixture-based migration test.
Missing CLI usage/status updates: no changes to `cli/cmd/agent_usage.go` or `cli/cmd/status.go` to stop reading legacy `sessions.jsonl`.
Missing performance validation: no benchmark or fixture proving 10,000 index entries resolve within the required threshold.
Missing UI gap/banner behavior and Playwright coverage for `/sessions`, lazy loading, migration, and live feed updates.

.ddx/executions/20260423T005714-24085f34/prompt.md:5:## Observed

Per-project `/beads` page is noticeably slow; the cross-project "all beads" view is substantially slower. Related: `ddx-ad0db8fd` already identified the single-bead-detail slowness caused by the same storage pattern.

## Root cause (same pattern, different call site)

`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_beads.go:22,40` —

- `Beads` (cross-project) and `BeadsByProject` (single-project) both call `r.State.GetBeadSnapshots(status, label, projectID, search)`.
- `GetBeadSnapshots` (`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:49-105`) iterates every registered project, constructs a `bead.Store` per project, calls `ReadAll()` (scan + JSONL parse of every bead file), filters in Go. Cost is `O(projects × beads_per_project)` on every call, independent of the filter/page size.
- All filtering (status, label, project, search) is Go-side, post-read. The page-size window is applied in `beadConnectionFromSnapshots` after the full corpus is already materialized.
- Cross-project "all beads" = same loop, just without the projectID filter. It is the worst case by design.

This is the same shape that's breaking the Sessions feed (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) and single-bead detail (`ddx-ad0db8fd`). A point fix here is insufficient — we need a measurement surface first so we stop relying on anecdote.

## Proposed direction

### Part 1 — Perf harness (foundational; enables everything else)

Introduce a reusable GraphQL perf harness under `cli/internal/server/perf/` or `cli/internal/server/graphql/bench_test.go`:

- Seeded fixture builder: generates N projects × M beads with configurable distribution of status/labels/dependencies. Reuses existing store primitives (no fake data layer).
- Go benchmark + scriptable HTTP runner (wrapper around an already-running server) reporting p50/p95/p99 per query shape.
- Targets covered on day one: `bead(id:)`, `beadsByProject(projectID, first, after, status, label, search)`, `beads(projectID=nil, first, after, status, label)`, `docGraph`, `sessions` (so the sessions shard work `ddx-2ceb02fa` inherits this harness). New query targets are added by writing a single table row.
- Thresholds are **recorded, not asserted**, in the first pass. CI surfaces a regression if a threshold is breached beyond a configured delta (e.g., +25% p95). Initial pass is "land the harness and record baseline"; a follow-up wires it into CI.

### Part 2 — Fix the scan pattern

Minimum viable, in priority order:

1. **Scope by project when the query is scoped.** `BeadsByProject` should call a new `GetBeadSnapshotsForProject(projectID, filters…)` that only opens that one project's store. Trivial change; eliminates the cross-project factor for the common case.
2. **Early filter pushdown.** `GetBeadSnapshots` should apply status/label/projectID filters at the file-parse boundary where cheap, not after materializing every bead. (Still reads every file — deeper fix below.)
3. **Lightweight in-memory index with invalidation.** After (1) and (2), if "all beads" or search is still slow, introduce an index keyed `{projectID, beadID}` with basic metadata (status, labels, title, updatedAt). Refresh on bead write + debounced filesystem watch. Full-body reads stay lazy — the index never holds bodies. This is the same storage discipline we adopted for sessions in `ddx-2ceb02fa`.

The harness from Part 1 is the gate for choosing between (2) and (3); don't commit to the index without numbers.

## Out of scope

- Full-text search infrastructure (tracked separately if needed).
- Changing the on-disk bead format.
- Cross-node federation performance. Current concern is a single server reading a single workspace.

LOCKED DECISIONS (do not relitigate; bake into TD):
1. Deployment: separate axon-server, operator-managed. DDx does NOT spawn or supervise Axon. Document the .ddx/config.yaml shape + ddx doctor diagnostic.
2. Auth: localhost-only by default; ts-net for remote. Reuse ADR-006 tsnet integration.
3. Events storage: separate ddx_bead_events collection with event_of links (Option B). Two collections in Axon.

- Backup/DR: operator-managed via Axon's own backing-store DR; ddx bead export is the logical backup format

- Is there a server-side aggregation/computed-view for the ready-queue traversal pattern?
- Does Axon's ts-net story align with ADR-006 (Tailscale tsnet listener) or require something different?

Source material (consume in order):
1. /tmp/ddx-axon-backend-plan.md — full integration plan with Axon's external interface mapped + GraphQL pivot + locked decisions
2. Cross-repo tracking bead axon-82b6f7b2 — full DDx-on-axon adoption checklist (links to all required axon-side capabilities)

LOCKED DECISIONS (do not relitigate; bake into TD):
1. Deployment: separate axon-server, operator-managed. DDx does NOT spawn or supervise Axon.
2. Auth: localhost-only by default; ts-net for remote. Axon already supports ts-net per its serve.rs (Tailscale mode + LocalAPI whois + role tags). Reuses ADR-006.
3. Events storage: separate ddx_bead_events collection with event_of links (Option B). Two collections in Axon.
4. Offline posture: refuse all bead ops when Axon unreachable. No local cache, no WAL.
5. Wire transport: GraphQL only via genqlient-generated Go client. NOT gRPC. Subscriptions over WebSocket for live updates.

Cross-repo dependencies (tracked in axon-82b6f7b2):
- axon-05c1019d (P2): GraphQL pattern-query support — needed for single-query ddx bead ready/blocked. Day 1 ships with two-phase fallback.
- FEAT-013 secondary indexes (P1 Draft in axon): needed for status-field query performance at >1000 beads.
- FEAT-017 schema evolution (P1 Draft in axon): needed for clean DDx schema upgrades over time. Day 1 ships with DDx-side lazy migration on read.
- ts-net auth: ALREADY SHIPPED in axon. No coordination needed.
- GraphQL CRUD + subscriptions: ALREADY SHIPPED in axon.
- Multi-namespace/database: ALREADY SHIPPED.

Adoption gates (per axon-82b6f7b2):
- Alpha: requires axon-05c1019d + everything already-shipped. Acceptable to ship with table-scan perf.
- Beta: requires FEAT-013 (indexes) for >1000-bead performance.
- GA: requires FEAT-013 + FEAT-017 + axon-05c1019d all landed.

Defaults the TD can adopt without rechecking with operator (revisit only if implementation surfaces a problem):
- Schema versioning: DDx-side lazy migrate on read until FEAT-017 lands; switch to axon-native then.
- ddx bead ready Day 1: two-phase GraphQL query.
- Backup/DR: operator-managed via Axon's own backing-store DR.

Output: TD-<NNN>-axon-bead-backend.md with sections for: GraphQL schema mapping (DDx bead → Axon entity); operation mapping (DDx CLI → GraphQL queries/mutations/subscriptions); deployment + config; concurrency model (OCC via expectedVersion); migration plan; test plan; cross-repo coordination notes (links to axon-82b6f7b2 + FEAT-013/017).

They have their own status concepts — binary installed, credentials configured, rate-limit headroom — that don't map cleanly onto the endpoint-provider model.\n- **No quota:** No existing data source captures rolling token usage vs quota. Quota information varies wildly by harness: Claude Code has message / session headroom concepts from response headers; codex has rate-limit headers; endpoint providers have API quota per key. The page renders an instant first paint from cached status; liveness probes fire in background and patch rows as they return. No synchronous probe-wall on load.\n\n### Deliverable 2 — Token \u0026 quota fields\n\n- Extend the status payload (per row) with: `usage { tokensUsedLastHour, tokensUsedLast24h, requestsLastHour, requestsLast24h }` and `quota { ceilingTokens?, ceilingWindowSeconds?, remaining?, resetAt? }`. Any field may be null if the provider/harness doesn't expose it.\n- Data sources:\n  - **Endpoint providers:** aggregate from the sessions index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) filtered to the last 1h / 24h, grouped by provider. Quota ceilings come from any headers we capture during calls, plus optional static config.\n  - **Subprocess harnesses:** same sessions-index aggregation for usage. Quota — parse rate-limit headers from the harness output where available (Claude Code, codex both emit them). For harnesses that don't, leave quota null and render \"not reported.\"\n- Row gains two small inline displays: utilization bar (used / ceiling for the quota window) and a 7-day sparkline of tokens/hour.\n\n### Deliverable 3 — Trend view\n\n- Click into a row → detail page showing: 7-day and 30-day time-series of tokens/hour and requests/hour, with the quota ceiling overlaid. Aggregations come from the sessions index, bucketed server-side to avoid shipping raw rows.\n- Simple \"projected to hit quota in ~X hours/days\" callout computed from the slope of the last 24h — enough to answer \"are we on a trend to run out?\" without building a forecasting system.\n\n## Cross-bead dependencies\n\n- Depends on `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index) for any usage-rate data. If that bead isn't landed when this work starts, the usage/quota features stub to \"not available\" and ship purely with the unified-view rename + async liveness.\n- Fixture / perf guidance: per the standing directive recorded in `ddx-9ce6842a`, any aggregation here is tested against ≥1k session records. When I click into one, I see a 7-day trend so I can tell if I'm about to run out.\n\n**Acceptance criteria:**\n\n1. **Deliverable 1 — Unified view.**\n   - New `harnessStatuses` GraphQL resolver returns subprocess harnesses (claude, codex, gemini, agent) with: name, kind=`harness`, reachable, detail, lastCheckedAt, defaultForProfile.\n   - Existing `providerStatuses` shape gains `kind=\\\"endpoint\\\"` and `lastCheckedAt`.\n   - UI page loads within 500ms on a warm cache — it renders from last-known status **first**, then patches rows live as async probes return. .ddx/executions/20260423T031421-5a434cc0/prompt.md:5:## Observed

Three related issues on `/nodes/.../providers`:

1. **Loads slowly.** The page fires two GraphQL queries on mount — `providerStatuses` (which probes every configured provider for live connectivity) and `defaultRouteStatus` (which resolves the default route). Both are synchronous and serialized only by `Promise.all`. Each provider probe is a real network call.
2. **Missing harnesses.** The pane shows API-endpoint providers (qwen, minimax, etc. — anything wired through the service with an endpoint). It does not show subprocess-invoked harnesses like `claude` (Claude Code) or `codex`. That is accurate to the current `providerStatuses` resolver (`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_providers.go:15-50`, which calls `svc.ListProviders`) but not what an operator wants: the operator thinks of "where their agent work can run," which includes both endpoint providers AND subprocess harnesses.
3. **No quota visibility.** Operator need: see current token utilization rate + quota utilization per harness/provider, trend it, and use the trend to decide whether to throttle before hitting a cap.

## Root cause by area

- **Slow load:** `svc.ListProviders` probes every configured provider for liveness. Probes are serial within the service call, and the whole query blocks page render.
- **Missing harnesses:** Subprocess harnesses (claude, codex, gemini) are first-class in the CLI (`ddx agent list`, `ddx agent doctor`, `ddx agent check`) but are not surfaced via `providerStatuses`. They have their own status concepts — binary installed, credentials configured, rate-limit headroom — that don't map cleanly onto the endpoint-provider model.
- **No quota:** No existing data source captures rolling token usage vs quota. Quota information varies wildly by harness: Claude Code has message / session headroom concepts from response headers; codex has rate-limit headers; endpoint providers have API quota per key. There is no unified representation today.

## Proposed direction

Three deliverables, sequenced from easy to hard.

### Deliverable 1 — Unified "Agent endpoints" view (renames/rescopes the page)

- Rename the surface from "Providers" to **"Agent endpoints"** (or similar — confirm copy in the design note). The domain object is "a place where agent work can run," not "an API provider."
- Merge the data: `providerStatuses` stays as the source for endpoint providers. A new `harnessStatuses` resolver (read from `svc.ListHarnesses` / existing `ddx agent list` + `ddx agent doctor` machinery) surfaces subprocess harnesses. Page renders both in one table with a `kind` column (`endpoint` | `harness`) — or two tables if design prefers.
- Status fields common to both: name, kind, reachable (bool), detail (text), last-checked-at, default-for-profile (set of profile names).
- Asynchronous liveness. The page renders an instant first paint from cached status; liveness probes fire in background and patch rows as they return. No synchronous probe-wall on load.

### Deliverable 2 — Token & quota fields

- Extend the status payload (per row) with: `usage { tokensUsedLastHour, tokensUsedLast24h, requestsLastHour, requestsLast24h }` and `quota { ceilingTokens?, ceilingWindowSeconds?, remaining?, resetAt? }`. Any field may be null if the provider/harness doesn't expose it.
- Data sources:
  - **Endpoint providers:** aggregate from the sessions index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) filtered to the last 1h / 24h, grouped by provider. Quota ceilings come from any headers we capture during calls, plus optional static config.
  - **Subprocess harnesses:** same sessions-index aggregation for usage. Quota — parse rate-limit headers from the harness output where available (Claude Code, codex both emit them). For harnesses that don't, leave quota null and render "not reported."
- Row gains two small inline displays: utilization bar (used / ceiling for the quota window) and a 7-day sparkline of tokens/hour.

### Deliverable 3 — Trend view

- Click into a row → detail page showing: 7-day and 30-day time-series of tokens/hour and requests/hour, with the quota ceiling overlaid. Aggregations come from the sessions index, bucketed server-side to avoid shipping raw rows.
- Simple "projected to hit quota in ~X hours/days" callout computed from the slope of the last 24h — enough to answer "are we on a trend to run out?" without building a forecasting system.

## Cross-bead dependencies

- Depends on `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index) for any usage-rate data. If that bead isn't landed when this work starts, the usage/quota features stub to "not available" and ship purely with the unified-view rename + async liveness.
- Fixture / perf guidance: per the standing directive recorded in `ddx-9ce6842a`, any aggregation here is tested against ≥1k session records. If in-place aggregation can't hit perf targets, bring the DB-substrate question to the user before picking one.
.ddx/executions/20260423T031421-5a434cc0/prompt.md:8:**User story:** As an operator, I want a single view that shows every place my agents can run — endpoint providers and subprocess harnesses alike — with status, recent token usage, and quota utilization. When I click into one, I see a 7-day trend so I can tell if I'm about to run out.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Deliverable 1 — Unified view.**
   - New `harnessStatuses` GraphQL resolver returns subprocess harnesses (claude, codex, gemini, agent) with: name, kind=`harness`, reachable, detail, lastCheckedAt, defaultForProfile.
   - Existing `providerStatuses` shape gains `kind=\"endpoint\"` and `lastCheckedAt`.
   - UI page loads within 500ms on a warm cache — it renders from last-known status **first**, then patches rows live as async probes return. Playwright asserts the table is interactive within 500ms of navigation and that row status patches within 5s of the probe completing.
   - Subprocess harnesses claude and codex **appear in the table** on a machine where their binaries are installed, with correct reachable status. Integration test.

2. **Deliverable 2 — Usage + quota fields (stubbable).**
   - Per-row fields `usage` and `quota` as described. All fields nullable.

   - For subprocess harnesses that expose rate-limit headers (claude, codex), parsed headers populate `quota.ceilingTokens`, `quota.ceilingWindowSeconds`, `quota.remaining`, `quota.resetAt`. Parser tested with captured header fixtures from each harness.
   - For harnesses/providers that expose nothing, fields remain null and the UI renders `—` / "not reported"; no fabricated data.
   - Utilization bar renders when both `usage` and `quota.ceilingTokens` are present. Sparkline renders when ≥6 hourly buckets of usage are available.

   - Detail route `/nodes/.../providers/[name]` shows 7-day and 30-day tokens/hour + requests/hour time series, with quota ceiling overlay when known.
   - Buckets computed server-side from the sessions index; the client receives ≤ 200 data points per series.
   - "Projected to hit quota in ~X" callout uses last-24h slope only. Renders only when `quota.ceilingTokens` is known and the last-24h slope is non-zero positive; otherwise absent.
   - Playwright asserts the detail route renders with seeded usage and the projection text appears for a fixture that has a ceiling + ascending usage.

4. **Perf posture.**
   - Fixture for perf test: ≥1,000 session index rows covering the last 30 days with usage distributed across ≥5 endpoints/harnesses. Per standing directive in `ddx-9ce6842a`.
   - Targets: unified list p95 ≤ 200ms HTTP (excluding async probes); trend detail p95 ≤ 400ms HTTP on the fixture.
   - If in-place aggregation cannot hit the detail-view p95 on a 10,000-row stretch fixture, pause and raise the DB-substrate decision with the user per the standing directive — do not silently switch.

5. **Design note.**
   - One-pager in `docs/helix/02-design/` before implementation recording the page-rename decision, the row model (common fields, kind-specific fields), async-probe strategy, and the quota source per harness/provider. PR links to it.

6. **No regressions.**
   - `ddx agent providers`, `ddx agent doctor`, `ddx agent list`, `ddx agent check` CLI commands continue to work unchanged.
   - `defaultRouteStatus` query remains.

7. **Cross-references.**
   - Depends on `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index) for usage aggregation. If that bead is not yet landed when this work starts, ship Deliverable 1 standalone with usage/quota stubbed as "not available," and re-open the remaining deliverables when the index is ready.

.ddx/executions/20260423T035053-7b39eb3f/prompt.md:11:## 2026-04-22 — User-directed amendment: shard the index

Per user feedback: a single `sessions.index.jsonl` just replays the same problem at smaller scale. The aggregate needs to be split.

### Revised storage shape

- Directory: `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/` (per-project, under each registered project's `.ddx`).
- Inside: **monthly shards** named `sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`.
- Each shard is pointer-only, same fields as the previously-proposed index (id, projectID, beadID, harness, surface, model, startedAt, endedAt, durationMs, cost, tokens, outcome, exitCode, bundlePath, nativeLogRef).
- Writer resolves the target shard from `time.Now().UTC()` at append time and creates the shard file lazily on first write of the month.
- Heavy bodies (prompt, response, stderr) stay where they already live — `.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/` and agent-log streams — and are lazy-loaded by the `session(id:)` resolver.

### Why monthly, not daily / size-based / SQLite

- **Daily** — produces tens of thousands of files over the project lifetime; ReadDir becomes the cost.
- **Size-based** — requires coordinating rotation with the append; race-prone and hard to test.
- **SQLite** — overkill for first pass; revisit if a single monthly shard grows past tens of MB.
- **Monthly** — ≤12 shards/year, human-readable filenames, date-range queries map trivially to shard selection, bounded shard size in practice.

### Reader behavior

- Default Sessions query loads the **current + previous shard** (covers "recent activity" for any current date).
- Date-range queries map the range to a shard set and read only those.
- Pagination beyond the default window triggers older-shard loads on demand.
- No shard-coordination lock needed: each month's file is append-only and only the current-month shard is actively written.

### Legacy migration

- `ddx agent log reindex` reads the existing `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` once and splits by each entry's `timestamp` field into the appropriate monthly shards under `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/`.
- After migration, the legacy file is renamed `sessions.jsonl.legacy` (not deleted) and never read again by the server. Hand-removal is the user's call.
- Idempotent: re-running the command merges without duplicating (index row dedup by `id`).

### Revised acceptance criteria (supersedes criteria 1, 5, 6, 7 on original description)

1. **Sharded index writer.** Single shared call site (from `service.Execute` or equivalent wrapper) appends one line to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, computing the shard from `time.Now().UTC()`. Test: write across a simulated month boundary and assert two shards exist with the correct split.

5. **Legacy migration shards too.** `ddx agent log reindex` splits `sessions.jsonl` into monthly shards by entry timestamp. Integration test uses a fixture with entries across 3 distinct months and asserts 3 shards are produced with correct contents and no duplicates on re-run.

6. **Server reads the shard directory.** `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` reads `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/*.jsonl`, respects any date-range arg, and loads only the shards that intersect the range. Unit test seeds 6 monthly shards and asserts a 2-month query reads exactly 2 files.

7. **Perf on sharded index.** Default Sessions view (current + previous shard) resolves in ≤50ms p95 on a fixture with 12 monthly shards × 10k entries each (120k total). Capture baseline (single-file read of same corpus) vs sharded in bead notes.

(Criteria 2, 3, 4, 8, 9 from the original acceptance list remain as written.)

### Scope clarifier

This amendment does not change the single-call-site writer guarantee or the lazy-body resolver — those remain the load-bearing ideas. The amendment is specifically about **how the pointer index is physically stored**.
## 2026-04-23 — Implementation benchmark and evidence

Baseline: `go test ./internal/agent -run '^$' -bench 'Benchmark(ReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow|LegacySessionsJSONLFullScan)$' -benchtime=1x` measured legacy single-file full scan/sort of 120k legacy-shaped rows with 2KB embedded responses at `BenchmarkLegacySessionsJSONLFullScan-12 1 1731705957 ns/op`.

After fix: the same command measured the sharded default-window reader at `BenchmarkReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow-12 1 8103661 ns/op` for 12 monthly shards x 10k rows each (120k total), with the default query reading only the current + previous shard window. Environment footer: linux/arm64, Go go1.26.2.

Acceptance evidence: `TestSessionIndexWritesMonthlyShards`, `TestSessionIndexShardFilesSelectsOnlyIntersectingDateRange`, `TestReindexLegacySessionsSplitsShardsAndDedupes`, `TestRunViaServiceWithAppendsOneSessionIndexRow`, `TestProductionAgentExecutionPathsUseIndexedServiceWriter`, `TestGraphQLAgentSessions`, `TestAgentLogReindexMigratesLegacyFile`, existing agent usage/status/processmetrics tests, and `e2e/sessions.spec.ts` cover writer sharding, bounded reads, migration, lazy body loading, deprecated-file read removal, UI gap banner, and live refresh.

REVIEW:BLOCK

Missing session index writer: no changes to `service.Execute`, shared execution wrappers, or agent execution paths to append `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl`.
Missing GraphQL changes: no update to `cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go` for index-backed `sessions` or lazy `session(id:)` body loading.
Missing migration command: no `ddx agent log reindex` or equivalent CLI implementation and no fixture-based migration test.
Missing CLI usage/status updates: no changes to `cli/cmd/agent_usage.go` or `cli/cmd/status.go` to stop reading legacy `sessions.jsonl`.
Missing performance validation: no benchmark or fixture proving 10,000 index entries resolve within the required threshold.
Missing UI gap/banner behavior and Playwright coverage for `/sessions`, lazy loading, migration, and live feed updates.

REVIEW:BLOCK

**AC 1–3**: No `sessions.index.jsonl` schema, writer in `service.Execute`, or updated GraphQL resolver in `cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go`.
**AC 4**: No `session(id: ID!)` resolver for lazy-loading prompt/response/stderr from execution bundles.
**AC 5**: No `ddx agent log reindex` command or migration logic.
**AC 6**: `cli/cmd/agent_usage.go` and `cli/cmd/status.go` are untouched.
**AC 7**: No benchmark captured.
**AC 8–9**: No frontend changes or Playwright tests.

.ddx/executions/20260423T035055-06c99b23/prompt.md:5:## Observed

Per-project `/beads` page is noticeably slow; the cross-project "all beads" view is substantially slower. Related: `ddx-ad0db8fd` already identified the single-bead-detail slowness caused by the same storage pattern.

## Root cause (same pattern, different call site)

`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_beads.go:22,40` —

- `Beads` (cross-project) and `BeadsByProject` (single-project) both call `r.State.GetBeadSnapshots(status, label, projectID, search)`.
- `GetBeadSnapshots` (`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:49-105`) iterates every registered project, constructs a `bead.Store` per project, calls `ReadAll()` (scan + JSONL parse of every bead file), filters in Go. Cost is `O(projects × beads_per_project)` on every call, independent of the filter/page size.
- All filtering (status, label, project, search) is Go-side, post-read. The page-size window is applied in `beadConnectionFromSnapshots` after the full corpus is already materialized.
- Cross-project "all beads" = same loop, just without the projectID filter. It is the worst case by design.

This is the same shape that's breaking the Sessions feed (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) and single-bead detail (`ddx-ad0db8fd`). A point fix here is insufficient — we need a measurement surface first so we stop relying on anecdote.

## Proposed direction

### Part 1 — Perf harness (foundational; enables everything else)

Introduce a reusable GraphQL perf harness under `cli/internal/server/perf/` or `cli/internal/server/graphql/bench_test.go`:

- Seeded fixture builder: generates N projects × M beads with configurable distribution of status/labels/dependencies. Reuses existing store primitives (no fake data layer).
- Go benchmark + scriptable HTTP runner (wrapper around an already-running server) reporting p50/p95/p99 per query shape.
- Targets covered on day one: `bead(id:)`, `beadsByProject(projectID, first, after, status, label, search)`, `beads(projectID=nil, first, after, status, label)`, `docGraph`, `sessions` (so the sessions shard work `ddx-2ceb02fa` inherits this harness). New query targets are added by writing a single table row.
- Thresholds are **recorded, not asserted**, in the first pass. CI surfaces a regression if a threshold is breached beyond a configured delta (e.g., +25% p95). Initial pass is "land the harness and record baseline"; a follow-up wires it into CI.

### Part 2 — Fix the scan pattern

Minimum viable, in priority order:

1. **Scope by project when the query is scoped.** `BeadsByProject` should call a new `GetBeadSnapshotsForProject(projectID, filters…)` that only opens that one project's store. Trivial change; eliminates the cross-project factor for the common case.
2. **Early filter pushdown.** `GetBeadSnapshots` should apply status/label/projectID filters at the file-parse boundary where cheap, not after materializing every bead. (Still reads every file — deeper fix below.)
3. **Lightweight in-memory index with invalidation.** After (1) and (2), if "all beads" or search is still slow, introduce an index keyed `{projectID, beadID}` with basic metadata (status, labels, title, updatedAt). Refresh on bead write + debounced filesystem watch. Full-body reads stay lazy — the index never holds bodies. This is the same storage discipline we adopted for sessions in `ddx-2ceb02fa`.

The harness from Part 1 is the gate for choosing between (2) and (3); don't commit to the index without numbers.

## Out of scope

- Full-text search infrastructure (tracked separately if needed).
- Changing the on-disk bead format.
- Cross-node federation performance. Current concern is a single server reading a single workspace.

- Date-range queries map the range to a shard set and read only those.
- Pagination beyond the default window triggers older-shard loads on demand.
- No shard-coordination lock needed: each month's file is append-only and only the current-month shard is actively written.

### Legacy migration

- `ddx agent log reindex` reads the existing `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl` once and splits by each entry's `timestamp` field into the appropriate monthly shards under `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/`.
- After migration, the legacy file is renamed `sessions.jsonl.legacy` (not deleted) and never read again by the server. Hand-removal is the user's call.
- Idempotent: re-running the command merges without duplicating (index row dedup by `id`).

### Revised acceptance criteria (supersedes criteria 1, 5, 6, 7 on original description)

1. **Sharded index writer.** Single shared call site (from `service.Execute` or equivalent wrapper) appends one line to `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`, computing the shard from `time.Now().UTC()`. Test: write across a simulated month boundary and assert two shards exist with the correct split.

5. **Legacy migration shards too.** `ddx agent log reindex` splits `sessions.jsonl` into monthly shards by entry timestamp. Integration test uses a fixture with entries across 3 distinct months and asserts 3 shards are produced with correct contents and no duplicates on re-run.

6. **Server reads the shard directory.** `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` reads `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/*.jsonl`, respects any date-range arg, and loads only the shards that intersect the range. Unit test seeds 6 monthly shards and asserts a 2-month query reads exactly 2 files.

7. **Perf on sharded index.** Default Sessions view (current + previous shard) resolves in ≤50ms p95 on a fixture with 12 monthly shards × 10k entries each (120k total). Capture baseline (single-file read of same corpus) vs sharded in bead notes.

(Criteria 2, 3, 4, 8, 9 from the original acceptance list remain as written.)

### Scope clarifier

This amendment does not change the single-call-site writer guarantee or the lazy-body resolver — those remain the load-bearing ideas. The amendment is specifically about **how the pointer index is physically stored**.
## 2026-04-23 — Implementation benchmark and evidence

Baseline: `go test ./internal/agent -run '^$' -bench 'Benchmark(ReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow|LegacySessionsJSONLFullScan)$' -benchtime=1x` measured legacy single-file full scan/sort of 120k legacy-shaped rows with 2KB embedded responses at `BenchmarkLegacySessionsJSONLFullScan-12 1 1731705957 ns/op`.

After fix: the same command measured the sharded default-window reader at `BenchmarkReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow-12 1 8103661 ns/op` for 12 monthly shards x 10k rows each (120k total), with the default query reading only the current + previous shard window. Environment footer: linux/arm64, Go go1.26.2.

Acceptance evidence: `TestSessionIndexWritesMonthlyShards`, `TestSessionIndexShardFilesSelectsOnlyIntersectingDateRange`, `TestReindexLegacySessionsSplitsShardsAndDedupes`, `TestRunViaServiceWithAppendsOneSessionIndexRow`, `TestProductionAgentExecutionPathsUseIndexedServiceWriter`, `TestGraphQLAgentSessions`, `TestAgentLogReindexMigratesLegacyFile`, existing agent usage/status/processmetrics tests, and `e2e/sessions.spec.ts` cover writer sharding, bounded reads, migration, lazy body loading, deprecated-file read removal, UI gap banner, and live refresh.

.ddx/executions/20260423T044433-ce3a2c9d/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Observed\n\nThree related issues on `/nodes/.../providers`:\n\n1. **Loads slowly.** The page fires two GraphQL queries on mount — `providerStatuses` (which probes every configured provider for live connectivity) and `defaultRouteStatus` (which resolves the default route). Both are synchronous and serialized only by `Promise.all`. Each provider probe is a real network call.\n2. **Missing harnesses.** The pane shows API-endpoint providers (qwen, minimax, etc. — anything wired through the service with an endpoint). It does not show subprocess-invoked harnesses like `claude` (Claude Code) or `codex`. That is accurate to the current `providerStatuses` resolver (`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_providers.go:15-50`, which calls `svc.ListProviders`) but not what an operator wants: the operator thinks of \"where their agent work can run,\" which includes both endpoint providers AND subprocess harnesses.\n3. **No quota visibility.** Operator need: see current token utilization rate + quota utilization per harness/provider, trend it, and use the trend to decide whether to throttle before hitting a cap.\n\n## Root cause by area\n\n- **Slow load:** `svc.ListProviders` probes every configured provider for liveness. Probes are serial within the service call, and the whole query blocks page render.\n- **Missing harnesses:** Subprocess harnesses (claude, codex, gemini) are first-class in the CLI (`ddx agent list`, `ddx agent doctor`, `ddx agent check`) but are not surfaced via `providerStatuses`. They have their own status concepts — binary installed, credentials configured, rate-limit headroom — that don't map cleanly onto the endpoint-provider model.\n- **No quota:** No existing data source captures rolling token usage vs quota. Quota information varies wildly by harness: Claude Code has message / session headroom concepts from response headers; codex has rate-limit headers; endpoint providers have API quota per key. There is no unified representation today.\n\n## Proposed direction\n\nThree deliverables, sequenced from easy to hard.\n\n### Deliverable 1 — Unified \"Agent endpoints\" view (renames/rescopes the page)\n\n- Rename the surface from \"Providers\" to **\"Agent endpoints\"** (or similar — confirm copy in the design note). The domain object is \"a place where agent work can run,\" not \"an API provider.\"\n- Merge the data: `providerStatuses` stays as the source for endpoint providers. A new `harnessStatuses` resolver (read from `svc.ListHarnesses` / existing `ddx agent list` + `ddx agent doctor` machinery) surfaces subprocess harnesses. Page renders both in one table with a `kind` column (`endpoint` | `harness`) — or two tables if design prefers.\n- Status fields common to both: name, kind, reachable (bool), detail (text), last-checked-at, default-for-profile (set of profile names).\n- Asynchronous liveness. The page renders an instant first paint from cached status; liveness probes fire in background and patch rows as they return. No synchronous probe-wall on load.\n\n### Deliverable 2 — Token \u0026 quota fields\n\n- Extend the status payload (per row) with: `usage { tokensUsedLastHour, tokensUsedLast24h, requestsLastHour, requestsLast24h }` and `quota { ceilingTokens?, ceilingWindowSeconds?, remaining?, resetAt? }`. Any field may be null if the provider/harness doesn't expose it.\n- Data sources:\n  - **Endpoint providers:** aggregate from the sessions index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`) filtered to the last 1h / 24h, grouped by provider. Quota ceilings come from any headers we capture during calls, plus optional static config.\n  - **Subprocess harnesses:** same sessions-index aggregation for usage. Quota — parse rate-limit headers from the harness output where available (Claude Code, codex both emit them). For harnesses that don't, leave quota null and render \"not reported.\"\n- Row gains two small inline displays: utilization bar (used / ceiling for the quota window) and a 7-day sparkline of tokens/hour.\n\n### Deliverable 3 — Trend view\n\n- Click into a row → detail page showing: 7-day and 30-day time-series of tokens/hour and requests/hour, with the quota ceiling overlaid. Aggregations come from the sessions index, bucketed server-side to avoid shipping raw rows.\n- Simple \"projected to hit quota in ~X hours/days\" callout computed from the slope of the last 24h — enough to answer \"are we on a trend to run out?\" without building a forecasting system.\n\n## Cross-bead dependencies\n\n- Depends on `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index) for any usage-rate data.
